ORACLE DATABASE ARCHITECTURE
PROCESS · MEMORY · DATA FLOW · STORAGE — CLICK ANY COMPONENT
◈ SQL CONVERTER
?
Not logged in
--
CLIENT / NETWORK LAYER
ORACLE INSTANCE — SYSTEM GLOBAL AREA (SGA) + PGA
BACKGROUND PROCESSES — MEMORY ↔ DISK BRIDGE
ORACLE DATABASE — PHYSICAL STORAGE (DISK / ASM)
CLIENT APPLICATION
JDBC · ODBC · OCI
SQL*PLUS · APP SERVER
LISTENER
PORT 1521
TNS RESOLVE
⚙
SERVER PROCESS
+ PGA
Private mem per session
Sort Area · Hash Area
Cursor State · Session Stack
Bind Variable values

Spawned by LISTENER on connect
1 OS process per session
(Dedicated Server mode)
≈ SQL Server: worker thread
memory (per-session stack)
PER SESSION
DATABASE BUFFER CACHE
8KB blocks from disk, cached in RAM
LRU eviction · Clean/Dirty buffers
Hash chains by DBA (file#+block#)
≈ SQL Server: Buffer Pool
(same LRU concept, 8KB pages)
🟩 CLEAN🟥 DIRTYLRU ORDER →
DB_CACHE_SIZE · KEEP · RECYCLE POOLS
SHARED POOL
LIBRARY CACHE
Parsed SQL · PL/SQL code
Execution plans · Cursors
Hard parse ↔ Soft parse
≈ SQL Server: Plan Cache
DATA DICT CACHE (ROW CACHE)
Table/Column metadata
Privilege info · Object IDs
≈ SQL Server: sys.objects cache
RESULT CACHE · JAVA POOL
REDO LOG BUFFER
REDO
RING
Circular fixed-size buffer
Change vectors: before/after
Filled by server processes
Drained by LGWR to disk
LOG_BUFFER = 512KB–16MB
≈ SQL Server: Log Buffer
(same WAL concept, 60KB)
LGWR FLUSH TRIGGERS:
▸ COMMIT ▸ 1/3 FULL ▸ 3 SEC
LARGE POOL
RMAN I/O buffers
Parallel query msgs
Shared Server (UGA)
≈ SQL Server: no direct
equivalent (uses Buffer Pool)
STREAMS POOL
GoldenGate capture
Advanced Queuing
LogMiner buffers
≈ SQL Server: CDC/Replication
memory (from Buffer Pool)
JAVA POOL
Java class data
Java stored procs
JVM session state
≈ SQL Server: CLR memory
(loaded in AppDomain)
FIXED SGA
Internal pointers
Lock structures
V$ view data · X$ tables
≈ SQL Server: internal
SQLOS memory structures
LGWR
LOG WRITER

Redo Buffer → Redo Logs
Sequential I/O
COMMIT gatekeeper
Fastest background proc
≈ SQL Server: Log Writer
thread (writes log buffer)
ON COMMIT
DBWn
DB WRITER

Dirty bufs → Data Files
Scattered async I/O
Checkpoint driven
Up to 20 writers
≈ SQL Server: Lazy Writer
+ Checkpoint (flush dirty)
CHECKPOINT
CKPT
CHECKPOINT

Signals DBWn to write
Updates file headers
Updates control file
Advances checkpoint SCN
≈ SQL Server: Checkpoint
process (same concept)
PERIODIC
ARCn
ARCHIVER

Redo Logs → Archive
After log switch
Enables PITR recovery
Multiple: ARC0..ARC9
≈ SQL Server: Log Shipping
copy job (backup+copy TRN)
LOG SWITCH
SMON
SYS MONITOR

Instance crash recovery
TEMP seg cleanup
Dead txn rollback
Free space coalesce
≈ SQL Server: Recovery at
startup (redo/undo phase)
STARTUP+IDLE
PMON
PROC MONITOR

Dead process cleanup
Rollback dead TX
Release all locks
Free PGA memory
≈ SQL Server: no equivalent
(Engine handles internally)
ON DISCONNECT
MMON
MANAGEABILITY

AWR snapshots every 1hr
Metric capture (ASH)
Alert threshold checks
ADDM triggers
≈ SQL Server: Query Store +
Perf Data Collector (DMVs)
EVERY 1HR
MMAN
MEM MANAGER

ASMM auto-tunes SGA
Grows/shrinks BC, SP
Monitors SGA pressure
AMM: SGA+PGA total
≈ SQL Server: Dynamic Memory
Manager (auto max server mem)
CONTINUOUS
RECO
Distributed TX
recovery (2PC)
≈ MSDTC
CJQ0
Scheduler job
queue process
≈ SQL Agent
DATA FILES (.DBF)
Physical storage · Tablespaces
Segments → Extents → 8KB Blocks
≈ SQL Server: .MDF/.NDF files
(Filegroups ↔ Tablespaces)
SYSTEM.DBF
SYSAUX.DBF
USERS01.DBF
APP_DATA01.DBF
UNDOTBS01.DBF
Tablespace Types:
▸ SYSTEM — data dictionary
▸ SYSAUX — AWR/EM repository
▸ USERS — default user data
▸ APP_* — application data
▸ INDEX_* — index segments
▸ LOB_* — BLOB/CLOB storage
DBWn writes dirty 8KB blocks here
Server process reads blocks from here
CKPT updates file headers (SCN)
ONLINE REDO LOGS
WAL journal · Circular · LGWR writes
≈ SQL Server: Transaction Log (.LDF)
— same WAL concept, but not circular
GROUP 1 ★ CURRENT
REDO01a.LOG · REDO01b.LOG (mirrored)
GROUP 2
REDO02a.LOG · REDO02b.LOG
GROUP 3
REDO03a.LOG · REDO03b.LOG
LOG SWITCH: GRP1→GRP2→GRP3→GRP1
LGWR fills current group sequentially
ARCn copies full group → archive dest
Minimum 2 groups, recommend 3+
CONTROL FILE
DB name · Current SCN · Checkpoint SCN
Datafile list · Redo log locations
RMAN backup catalog · Log sequence#
CKPT updates on every checkpoint event
≈ SQL Server: master DB + boot page
(no single-file equivalent)
ARCHIVED REDO LOGS
ARCn copies here after log switch
≈ SQL Server: Transaction Log
Backups (.TRN files)
ARCHIVELOG MODE required
Archive dest 1 (local)
Archive dest 2 (Data Guard)
Enables Point-In-Time Recovery
RMAN uses for backup sets
UNDO TABLESPACE
Before-images of changed rows
ROLLBACK: undo transaction changes
MVCC: read consistency snapshots
ORA-01555 if undo retention exceeded
Managed by SMON + AUM (9i+)
≈ SQL Server: TempDB version
store (RCSI) + TLog rollback
TEMP TABLESPACE
Sort spill (ORDER BY, GROUP BY)
Hash join build side overflow
Index build (CREATE INDEX)
Global temp table data
Rebuilt at instance restart
Not archived / not backed up
Server proc reads/writes directly
≈ SQL Server: TempDB (same
purpose — spills, sorts, #temp)
SYSAUX / AWR
WRH$_SYSSTAT — system stats history
WRH$_ACTIVE_SESSION_HISTORY (ASH)
WRH$_SQL_PLAN — plan history
WRH$_SQLSTAT — SQL execution stats
Enterprise Manager repository
Oracle Text · Spatial metadata
MMON writes AWR snapshots here
≈ SQL Server: Query Store +
msdb (perf history/metadata)
SPFILE / PFILE
Instance startup parameters
DB_NAME · SGA_TARGET
DB_BLOCK_SIZE · LOG_BUFFER
UNDO_TABLESPACE · PROCESSES
Read at STARTUP (NOMOUNT state)
ALTER SYSTEM...SCOPE=SPFILE
Lives in $ORACLE_HOME/dbs/
≈ SQL Server: sp_configure +
sys.configurations (instance params)
ALERT LOG + TRACE
alert_ORCL.log — all errors
Checkpoint events logged
Log switch events
DB startup/shutdown
ORA- error messages
Background proc trace files
ADR: $ORACLE_BASE/diag/
≈ SQL Server: ERRORLOG +
Extended Events (.xel files)
PASSWORD
FILE
orapwORCL
SYS auth
SYSDBA/
SYSOPER
Remote DBA
connections

orapwd
utility creates
this file

≈ SQL Server: SA login
+ Windows Auth
REDO RECORDS → LGWR
SEQUENTIAL WRITE
DIRTY BLOCKS → DBWn
SCATTERED WRITE
COPY ON LOG SWITCH →
RECOVERY: REDO APPLY
AWR SNAPSHOT WRITE
CONTAINER DATABASE (CDB)
ORACLE 12c+ MULTITENANT ARCHITECTURE — ONE INSTANCE · MANY DATABASES
CON_ID=0
SHARED SGA — ALL PDBs USE THIS SINGLE INSTANCE SGA
DATABASE BUFFER CACHE
Shared 8KB block cache for ALL PDBs
PDB-level memory limits (12.2+)
DB_CACHE_SIZE shared across containers
Each PDB can set MAX_SIZE limit
SHARED POOL
Library Cache — SQL plans per PDB
Data Dict Cache — metadata per PDB
Each PDB has own namespace
Cursors tagged with CON_ID
REDO LOG BUFFER
Single shared redo stream
All PDB changes go here
LGWR writes for ALL containers
Each redo record tagged CON_ID
LARGE POOL
RMAN buffers (shared)
Parallel Query msgs
Shared Server UGA
PGA (PER SESSION)
Private per-session memory
Session knows its CON_ID
PGA_AGGREGATE_TARGET shared
PDB can limit via SGA_TARGET
SHARED BACKGROUND PROCESSES — ONE SET SERVES ALL PDBs
LGWR
DBWn
CKPT
ARCn
SMON
PMON
MMON
MMAN
CJQ0
ALL processes are CDB-wide · No per-PDB background processes
PLUGGABLE DATABASES — EACH PDB IS AN ISOLATED DATABASE WITHIN THE CDB
CDB$ROOT
CON_ID=1
Root container — houses the
master data dictionary for ALL
containers in the CDB.

Contains:
▸ SYSTEM tablespace (shared DD)
▸ SYSAUX tablespace (AWR/EM)
▸ UNDO tablespace (shared)
▸ Online Redo Logs (shared)
▸ Control Files (shared)
▸ TEMP tablespace (shared)

Common Users:
▸ SYS, SYSTEM (C## prefix)
▸ C##DBA_ADMIN
▸ Visible in ALL PDBs

Common Objects:
▸ Common roles/profiles
▸ Common audit policies
▸ Object links to PDBs
SYSTEM
SYSAUX
UNDO
REDO
PDB$SEED
CON_ID=2
READ-ONLY template PDB

Used as the "gold image"
when creating new PDBs.

CREATE PLUGGABLE
DATABASE pdb3
ADMIN USER pdb3admin
IDENTIFIED BY pass;

Oracle copies PDB$SEED's
data files to create the
new PDB's initial set.

Cannot be modified.
Cannot be dropped.
Always present in CDB.

Status: READ ONLY
Open Mode: MOUNTED
SYSTEM
SYSAUX
PDB1 — HR_APPLICATION
CON_ID=3
Fully isolated application database

▸ Own SYSTEM + SYSAUX tablespaces
▸ Own TEMP tablespace (optional)
▸ Own application data files
▸ Own local users (HR, APP_USER)
▸ Own schemas, tables, indexes
▸ Separate service name for connections

Connection:
sqlplus hr/pass@//host:1521/pdb1

PDB Operations:
ALTER PLUGGABLE DATABASE pdb1 OPEN;
ALTER PLUGGABLE DATABASE pdb1 CLOSE;

Portability:
ALTER PLUGGABLE DATABASE pdb1
  UNPLUG INTO '/tmp/pdb1.xml';

Can be unplugged and plugged
into a different CDB — full
database portability!
SYSTEM
SYSAUX
HR_DATA
HR_IDX
PDB2 — SALES_APPLICATION
CON_ID=4
Fully isolated application database

▸ Own SYSTEM + SYSAUX tablespaces
▸ Own TEMP tablespace (optional)
▸ Own application data files
▸ Own local users (SALES, ORDERS)
▸ Own schemas, tables, indexes
▸ Separate service name for connections

Connection:
sqlplus sales/pass@//host:1521/pdb2

Resource Limits (12.2+):
ALTER PLUGGABLE DATABASE pdb2
  SET MAX_CPU = 2;
ALTER PLUGGABLE DATABASE pdb2
  SET MAX_SGA_SIZE = 4G;

Isolation:
PDB1 users CANNOT see PDB2 data
Complete namespace isolation
Separate AWR data per PDB (21c+)
SYSTEM
SYSAUX
SALES_DATA
ORDER_IDX
MAX 4,098 PDBs per CDB (Oracle 21c)
ALTER SESSION SET CONTAINER = pdb1;
V$PDBS · CDB_DATA_FILES · DBA_PDBS
UNPLUG → XML Manifest + Data Files → PLUG into new CDB
ORACLE DATA GUARD
DISASTER RECOVERY — PRIMARY + PHYSICAL STANDBY WITH REAL-TIME REDO SHIPPING
MAX AVAILABILITY
OBSERVER
FAST-START FAILOVER (FSFO)
PRIMARY DATABASE
READ-WRITE · PRODUCTION · DB_UNIQUE_NAME = ORCL
OPEN READ WRITE
INSTANCE SGA
BUFFER CACHE
Data blocks cached
SHARED POOL
SQL plans + Dict cache
REDO LOG BUFFER
Changes before disk write
BACKGROUND PROCESSES
LGWR DBWn ARCn NSSn CKPT SMON PMON
PHYSICAL STORAGE
DATA FILES
SYSTEM · SYSAUX
USERS · APP_DATA
ONLINE REDO
Group 1 · 2 · 3
LGWR writes here
ARCHIVE LOGS
ARCn copies here
→ shipped to standby
CONTROL FILE
DB registry
Multiplexed x3
REDO TRANSPORT:
▸ LGWR ASYNC — sends redo via NSSn as generated (real-time)
▸ ARCn — ships archived logs after log switch (not real-time)
▸ LOG_ARCHIVE_DEST_2 = 'SERVICE=standby ASYNC VALID_FOR=(ONLINE_LOGFILES,PRIMARY_ROLE)'
▸ LOG_ARCHIVE_DEST_STATE_2 = ENABLE
REDO TRANSPORT
LGWR ASYNC → NSSn → NETWORK → RFS
ARCn → ARCHIVED REDO → NETWORK → RFS
Oracle Net Services · TCP/IP
Encrypted with Oracle Advanced Security
DATA GUARD BROKER
DGMGRL · Automated management · Switchover/Failover
MAX AVAILABILITY
MAX PERFORMANCE
MAX PROTECTION
PHYSICAL STANDBY
READ-ONLY (Active Data Guard) · DB_UNIQUE_NAME = ORCL_STBY
MOUNTED / READ ONLY
INSTANCE SGA (RECOVERY MODE)
BUFFER CACHE
Blocks from redo apply
SHARED POOL
Active DG: read queries
STANDBY REDO LOGS
RFS writes redo here
STANDBY PROCESSES
RFS MRP0 LGWR DBWn ARCn SMON
PHYSICAL STORAGE (BLOCK-FOR-BLOCK COPY)
DATA FILES
Identical to primary
MRP applies redo here
STANDBY REDO
RFS writes here
Real-time apply
ARCHIVE LOGS
Local archives
Gap resolution
CONTROL FILE
Standby control
ALTER DATABASE
CREATE STANDBY
CONTROLFILE
REDO APPLY (MRP):
▸ RFS receives redo → writes to Standby Redo Logs
▸ MRP0 reads Standby Redo Logs → applies to Data Files
▸ REAL-TIME APPLY: MRP reads SRL as RFS writes (no wait)
▸ ALTER DATABASE RECOVER MANAGED STANDBY DATABASE USING CURRENT LOGFILE;
DG OPERATIONS:
▸ SWITCHOVER: planned role reversal (zero data loss)
▸ FAILOVER: unplanned (Observer auto with FSFO)
▸ DGMGRL> SWITCHOVER TO 'orcl_stby';
▸ Active Data Guard: read-only queries on standby
ORACLE RAC (REAL APPLICATION CLUSTERS)
MULTIPLE INSTANCES · ONE SHARED DATABASE · CACHE FUSION INTERCONNECT
CLUSTER MODE
SCAN LISTENER
scan-cluster:1521 · 3 SCAN VIPs · Load Balance
INSTANCE 1 (ORCL1)
THREAD 1 · NODE rac-node1 · OWN SGA + PROCESSES
INSTANCE 1 SGA (PRIVATE)
BUFFER CACHE
Local + GCS copies
CR blocks from node2
SHARED POOL
Local SQL plans
GES lock info
REDO BUFFER
Thread 1 redo
Own redo stream
BACKGROUND PROCESSES
LGWR DBWn CKPT SMON LMSn LMD LMON ARCn
LMS = GCS (block shipping) · LMD = GES (lock mgmt) · LMON = Global Monitor
PER-INSTANCE (THREAD 1)
REDO THREAD 1
UNDO TBS 1
Each instance has own
redo thread + UNDO tbs
for crash recovery isolation
INSTANCE 2 (ORCL2)
THREAD 2 · NODE rac-node2 · OWN SGA + PROCESSES
INSTANCE 2 SGA (PRIVATE)
BUFFER CACHE
Local + GCS copies
CR blocks from node1
SHARED POOL
Local SQL plans
GES lock info
REDO BUFFER
Thread 2 redo
Own redo stream
BACKGROUND PROCESSES
LGWR DBWn CKPT SMON LMSn LMD LMON ARCn
LMS = GCS (block shipping) · LMD = GES (lock mgmt) · LMON = Global Monitor
PER-INSTANCE (THREAD 2)
REDO THREAD 2
UNDO TBS 2
Each instance has own
redo thread + UNDO tbs
for crash recovery isolation
CACHE FUSION — PRIVATE INTERCONNECT
High-speed private network (InfiniBand / 10GbE+)
GCS (LMS) ships data blocks between instances on demand
GES (LMD) coordinates global enqueue / lock resources
LMON monitors cluster membership + node eviction (split-brain)
Wait events: gc cr request · gc current request · gc buffer busy
SHARED STORAGE — ALL INSTANCES ACCESS THE SAME DATABASE FILES VIA ASM
ORACLE ASM (AUTOMATIC STORAGE MANAGEMENT)
Volume manager + File system for Oracle
+DATA (NORMAL REDUNDANCY)
DATA FILES
CONTROL
UNDO x2
TEMP
Both instances read/write · ASM mirrors across failure groups
+FRA (FLASH RECOVERY AREA)
REDO x2
ARCHIVED
BACKUPS
Per-instance redo threads · Shared archive destination
CLUSTER COMPONENTS
VOTING DISK
Node membership · Split-brain resolution
OCR
Oracle Cluster Registry · Cluster config
CRS / GI
Cluster Ready Services · Grid Infrastructure
ASM INSTANCE
+ASM1, +ASM2 · Manages disk groups
gc cr request = Block shipped via Cache Fusion (consistent read)
srvctl start instance -d ORCL -i ORCL2
V$INSTANCE · GV$SESSION (global) · GV$SYSSTAT
ORACLE RMAN — BACKUP & RECOVERY ARCHITECTURE
RECOVERY MANAGER · BACKUP SETS · CHANNELS · FRA · FLASHBACK · BLOCK CHANGE TRACKING
RMAN CLIENT
Command-line interface
Interactive · Script · Stored
rman target / catalog rman@catdb
RECOVERY CATALOG
Centralized metadata DB
Stored scripts · Multi-target
RC_BACKUP_SET · RC_BACKUP_PIECE
TARGET DATABASE
Database being backed up
Control file = backup metadata
V$BACKUP_SET · V$BACKUP_PIECE
RMAN CHANNELS
Server processes performing backup/recovery I/O
CH1 DISK
ORA_DISK_1
CH2 DISK
ORA_DISK_2
CH3 SBT
ORA_SBT_TAPE_1
CH4 SBT
ORA_SBT_TAPE_2

BACKUP OPERATIONS
FULL / L0 INCR L1 DIFF INCR L1 CUM ARCHIVELOG SPFILE/CF

BACKUP INCREMENTAL LEVEL 1 DATABASE

RESTORE RECOVER PITR BLOCK MEDIA
RESTORE DATABASE → RECOVER DATABASE
RECOVER UNTIL TIME / SCN / SEQUENCE
BLOCKRECOVER · Tablespace PITR · OPEN RESETLOGS
STORAGE LAYER
FLASH RECOVERY AREA
DB_RECOVERY_FILE_DEST
Auto-managed space
Backups + Archivelogs + Flashback
142 / 200 GB (71%)

Compressed · Encrypted

SERVER-SIDE · PARALLEL · RESTARTABLE · NETWORK MODE · FILTERING · REMAPPING
EXPORT (EXPDP)
Server-side data unload
FULL · SCHEMA · TABLE modes
expdp hr SCHEMAS=hr DUMPFILE=hr.dmp
MASTER CONTROL (DM)
Job coordination process
Master Table · Job state
ATTACH / DETACH / STATUS
IMPORT (IMPDP)
Server-side data load
REMAP · NETWORK_LINK
impdp system DUMPFILE=hr.dmp

ORACLE DATABASE SECURITY ARCHITECTURE
AUTHENTICATION · PRIVILEGES · ROLES · AUDITING · TDE · VPD · DB VAULT · LABEL SECURITY
AUTHENTICATION
PASSWORD EXTERNAL KERBEROS PROXY
SHA-512 + PBKDF2 verifiers (12c+)
PRIVILEGES
SYSTEM OBJECT ADMIN OPTION
200+ system privs · GRANT/REVOKE
ROLES
DBA RESOURCE CONNECT CUSTOM
Named privilege groups · Hierarchical
UNIFIED AUDITING
Consolidated audit trail · AUDSYS schema
Audit policies · Fine-Grained Audit (FGA)
UNIFIED_AUDIT_TRAIL · CREATE AUDIT POLICY
PROFILES
Password policies · Resource limits
PASSWORD_VERIFY_FUNCTION
FAILED_LOGIN_ATTEMPTS · PASSWORD_LIFE_TIME
NETWORK SECURITY
SSL/TLS · Native encryption
Listener hardening
SQLNET.ENCRYPTION_SERVER=REQUIRED
DATA PROTECTION & ACCESS CONTROL LAYER
TRANSPARENT DATA ENCRYPTION
Encrypt data at rest
Wallet / Keystore · AES-256
Tablespace · Column encryption
VPD / ROW-LEVEL
Virtual Private Database
Transparent WHERE predicate
DBMS_RLS · SYS_CONTEXT
DATABASE VAULT
Separation of duties
Realms · Command rules
Block even DBA/SYS
LABEL SECURITY
Mandatory access control
Levels · Compartments
OLS policy framework
DATA REDACTION
Dynamic data masking
FULL · PARTIAL · RANDOM
DBMS_REDACT
ORACLE PERFORMANCE TUNING — DIAGNOSTICS & OPTIMIZATION ARCHITECTURE CLICK FOR OVERVIEW
AWR
AUTOMATIC WORKLOAD REPOSITORY
◈ Snapshots (60 min)◈ DBA_HIST_* Views ◈ System Statistics◈ SQL Statistics ◈ Baselines◈ SYSAUX Storage
ADDM
AUTOMATIC DATABASE DIAGNOSTIC MONITOR
◈ Auto-Analysis◈ Top-Down Method ◈ Impact Ranking◈ Recommendations ◈ DB Time Focus◈ MMON Triggered
ASH
ACTIVE SESSION HISTORY
◈ 1-Second Sampling◈ V$ASH Buffer ◈ Session State◈ Wait Events ◈ SQL_ID Capture◈ Blocking Chains
SQL TUNING ADVISOR
AUTOMATIC SQL OPTIMIZATION & SQL PLAN MANAGEMENT
◈ SQL Profiles◈ Index Advice◈ Plan Baselines (SPM) ◈ Stats Analysis◈ Restructuring◈ Auto SQL Tuning
OPTIMIZER & EXECUTION PLANS
COST-BASED OPTIMIZER — PLAN GENERATION
◈ CBO Cost Model◈ EXPLAIN PLAN◈ Adaptive Plans ◈ Join Methods◈ DBMS_STATS◈ DBMS_XPLAN
WAIT EVENT ANALYSIS
BOTTLENECK IDENTIFICATION — 12 WAIT CLASSES
◈ DB Time Model◈ User I/O Waits ◈ Concurrency Waits◈ Commit Waits ◈ Application Waits◈ System I/O Waits
MEMORY TUNING
SGA + PGA — AUTOMATIC MEMORY MANAGEMENT
◈ AMM (MEMORY_TARGET)◈ ASMM (SGA_TARGET) ◈ Buffer Cache Advice◈ PGA Advisory ◈ Shared Pool Advice◈ Auto-Tuning
I/O TUNING
STORAGE I/O PERFORMANCE OPTIMIZATION
◈ IOPS / Throughput◈ I/O Latency ◈ Async I/O◈ Direct Path I/O ◈ V$FILESTAT◈ ASM Striping
INDEX TUNING
ACCESS PATH OPTIMIZATION — INDEX STRATEGIES
◈ B-tree Indexes◈ Bitmap Indexes ◈ Function-Based◈ Composite ◈ Invisible Indexes◈ SQL Access Advisor
SQL TRACE & TKPROF
DETAILED SQL EXECUTION TRACING
◈ Event 10046◈ TKPROF Formatter ◈ Bind Variables◈ Wait Events ◈ V$SQL_MONITOR◈ DBMS_MONITOR
RESOURCE MANAGER
WORKLOAD MANAGEMENT — CPU & I/O CONTROL
◈ Resource Plans◈ Consumer Groups ◈ Plan Directives◈ CPU Shares ◈ I/O Rate Limiting◈ Auto-Switching
ORACLE NETWORKING — LISTENERS, CONNECTIONS & COMMUNICATION CLICK FOR OVERVIEW
ORACLE LISTENER
LSNRCTL — CONNECTION REQUEST HANDLER
◈ Port 1521 (TCP)◈ Dynamic Registration ◈ listener.ora◈ LREG Process ◈ Multi-Protocol◈ lsnrctl Utility
TNSNAMES & NAMING
CONNECT STRING RESOLUTION METHODS
◈ tnsnames.ora◈ Easy Connect ◈ LDAP Directory◈ NAMES.DIRECTORY_PATH ◈ Connect Descriptors◈ tnsping Utility
CONNECTION MANAGER
CMAN — MULTIPLEXING & ACCESS CONTROL
◈ Multiplexing◈ Access Control ◈ Cross-Network Proxy◈ cman.ora ◈ cmctl Utility◈ Session Sharing
SHARED SERVER
DISPATCHER-BASED CONNECTION SHARING
◈ Dispatchers◈ Request Queue◈ Response Queue ◈ Shared Processes◈ V$DISPATCHER◈ V$QUEUE
DEDICATED SERVER
ONE SERVER PROCESS PER CLIENT CONNECTION
◈ 1:1 Process Model◈ PGA per Session◈ Direct I/O ◈ Listener Handoff◈ Best Performance◈ Higher Memory
DATABASE SERVICES
SERVICE-BASED WORKLOAD MANAGEMENT
◈ DBMS_SERVICE◈ LREG Registration ◈ V$SERVICES◈ V$SERVICE_STATS ◈ RAC Mapping◈ Failover Config
SQLNET.ORA
CLIENT & SERVER NETWORK CONFIGURATION
◈ Naming Resolution◈ Authentication ◈ Encryption Config◈ Dead Connection ◈ Timeouts◈ Buffer Sizes
LOAD BALANCING
CLIENT-SIDE & SERVER-SIDE DISTRIBUTION
◈ Client-Side (CLB)◈ Server-Side (SSLB) ◈ REMOTE_LISTENER◈ CLB_GOAL ◈ Address Lists◈ SCAN Listener
CONNECTION FAILOVER
TAF, FCF & APPLICATION CONTINUITY
◈ TAF (Transparent)◈ FCF (Fast) ◈ App Continuity◈ FAILOVER=ON ◈ ONS Events◈ Session Replay
DRCP
DATABASE RESIDENT CONNECTION POOLING
◈ Pooled Servers◈ SERVER=POOLED ◈ Connection Class◈ V$CPOOL_STATS ◈ DBMS_CONN_POOL◈ Session Affinity
NETWORK ENCRYPTION
NATIVE ENCRYPTION & TLS/SSL
◈ NNE (No Certs)◈ TLS/SSL (Wallet) ◈ AES256 / AES192◈ SHA Integrity ◈ orapki Utility◈ TCPS Protocol
■ STARTUP & SHUTDOWN
STARTUP MODES
STARTUP -- Full open (normal) STARTUP NOMOUNT -- Instance only; for CREATE DB / recreate ctrl STARTUP MOUNT -- Instance + ctrl file; for RMAN restore STARTUP RESTRICT -- Open for DBAs only (RESTRICTED SESSION) STARTUP FORCE -- Abort then restart STARTUP PFILE=/tmp/my.ora -- Use text parameter file ALTER DATABASE MOUNT; -- Advance from NOMOUNT → MOUNT ALTER DATABASE OPEN; -- Advance from MOUNT → OPEN ALTER DATABASE OPEN READ ONLY; -- Standby / reporting
SHUTDOWN MODES
SHUTDOWN NORMAL -- Wait for sessions; checkpoint; safe SHUTDOWN IMMEDIATE -- Rollback txns; checkpoint; most common SHUTDOWN TRANSACTIONAL -- Wait for active txns only SHUTDOWN ABORT -- Kill; no checkpoint; needs recovery! -- SQL Server equivalent: -- IMMEDIATE ≈ SQL Server graceful shutdown -- ABORT ≈ killing the SQL Server process
INIT PARAMETER MANAGEMENT
SHOW PARAMETER pga_aggregate_target; ALTER SYSTEM SET pga_aggregate_target=2G SCOPE=BOTH; ALTER SYSTEM SET log_archive_dest_2='...' SCOPE=MEMORY; ALTER SYSTEM SET db_cache_size=1G SCOPE=SPFILE; CREATE PFILE='/tmp/init.ora' FROM SPFILE; CREATE SPFILE FROM PFILE='/tmp/init.ora'; SELECT name, value, description FROM v$parameter WHERE name LIKE '%sga%';
■ CONNECTION & SESSION MANAGEMENT
SQL*PLUS CONNECTION
sqlplus / as sysdba -- OS auth (local) sqlplus sys/pass@ORCL as sysdba -- TNS connect sqlplus user/pass@//host:1521/svc -- Easy Connect sqlplus user/pass@'(DESC...)' as sysdba -- Inside SQL*Plus: SET LINESIZE 200; SET PAGESIZE 50; SPOOL /tmp/output.log; SPOOL OFF; @/tmp/script.sql -- Run script file HOST ls -la -- Run OS command
SESSION CONTROL
ALTER SESSION SET NLS_DATE_FORMAT='YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS'; ALTER SESSION SET CURRENT_SCHEMA = hr; ALTER SESSION SET OPTIMIZER_MODE = FIRST_ROWS_10; ALTER SESSION SET SQL_TRACE = TRUE; -- Kill a session SELECT sid, serial#, username, status FROM v$session; ALTER SYSTEM KILL SESSION '123,456' IMMEDIATE; ALTER SYSTEM DISCONNECT SESSION '123,456' POST_TRANSACTION;
SESSION MONITORING
SELECT sid, serial#, username, status, machine, sql_id, event, wait_class, seconds_in_wait FROM v$session WHERE status='ACTIVE'; SELECT sql_id, sql_text, executions, elapsed_time/1e6 sec FROM v$sql ORDER BY elapsed_time DESC FETCH FIRST 10 ROWS ONLY; SELECT blocking_session, sid, wait_class, seconds_in_wait FROM v$session WHERE blocking_session IS NOT NULL;
■ USER, PRIVILEGE & SECURITY
USER LIFECYCLE
CREATE USER scott IDENTIFIED BY tiger DEFAULT TABLESPACE users TEMPORARY TABLESPACE temp QUOTA UNLIMITED ON users QUOTA 500M ON app_data; ALTER USER scott IDENTIFIED BY newpass; ALTER USER scott ACCOUNT LOCK; ALTER USER scott ACCOUNT UNLOCK; ALTER USER scott PASSWORD EXPIRE; ALTER USER scott QUOTA 0 ON users; -- revoke quota DROP USER scott CASCADE; -- drops all objects
GRANTS & ROLES
GRANT CREATE SESSION TO scott; GRANT DBA TO scott; -- full access GRANT SELECT ON hr.employees TO scott; GRANT SELECT, INSERT ON hr.emp TO scott WITH GRANT OPTION; GRANT EXECUTE ON dbms_stats TO scott; REVOKE SELECT ON hr.employees FROM scott; CREATE ROLE app_role; GRANT SELECT ANY TABLE TO app_role; GRANT app_role TO scott; GRANT app_role TO scott WITH ADMIN OPTION; -- Query effective privs SELECT * FROM session_privs; SELECT * FROM dba_sys_privs WHERE grantee='SCOTT';
PROFILES & AUDITING
CREATE PROFILE secure_profile LIMIT FAILED_LOGIN_ATTEMPTS 5 PASSWORD_LOCK_TIME 1/24 -- 1 hour PASSWORD_LIFE_TIME 90 PASSWORD_REUSE_TIME 365 PASSWORD_REUSE_MAX 5 SESSIONS_PER_USER 10 CPU_PER_SESSION UNLIMITED; ALTER USER scott PROFILE secure_profile; -- Unified Auditing (12c+) AUDIT SELECT ON hr.employees BY scott; AUDIT CREATE TABLE BY scott; SELECT * FROM unified_audit_trail WHERE db_username='SCOTT';
■ TABLESPACE & STORAGE MANAGEMENT
CREATE & ALTER TABLESPACE
CREATE TABLESPACE app_data DATAFILE '/u01/oradata/ORCL/app01.dbf' SIZE 1G AUTOEXTEND ON NEXT 256M MAXSIZE 20G EXTENT MANAGEMENT LOCAL SEGMENT SPACE MANAGEMENT AUTO; ALTER TABLESPACE app_data ADD DATAFILE '/u01/oradata/ORCL/app02.dbf' SIZE 1G; ALTER DATABASE DATAFILE '/u01/oradata/ORCL/app01.dbf' RESIZE 5G; ALTER TABLESPACE app_data OFFLINE; ALTER TABLESPACE app_data ONLINE; ALTER TABLESPACE app_data READ ONLY; ALTER TABLESPACE app_data READ WRITE; DROP TABLESPACE app_data INCLUDING CONTENTS AND DATAFILES;
TEMP & UNDO TABLESPACE
CREATE TEMPORARY TABLESPACE temp2 TEMPFILE '/u01/oradata/ORCL/temp02.dbf' SIZE 2G AUTOEXTEND ON NEXT 512M MAXSIZE UNLIMITED; ALTER DATABASE DEFAULT TEMPORARY TABLESPACE temp2; CREATE UNDO TABLESPACE undo2 DATAFILE '/u01/oradata/ORCL/undo02.dbf' SIZE 4G AUTOEXTEND ON NEXT 256M MAXSIZE UNLIMITED RETENTION GUARANTEE; ALTER SYSTEM SET UNDO_TABLESPACE = undo2; ALTER SYSTEM SET UNDO_RETENTION = 3600; -- seconds
STORAGE MONITORING
SELECT tablespace_name, bytes/1024/1024 "MB", maxbytes/1024/1024 "MAX_MB", autoextensible FROM dba_data_files ORDER BY 1; SELECT tablespace_name, round(used_space*8192/1024/1024,1) "USED_MB", round(tablespace_size*8192/1024/1024,1) "TOTAL_MB", round(used_percent,1) "PCT_USED" FROM dba_tablespace_usage_metrics; SELECT segment_name, segment_type, bytes/1024/1024 "MB" FROM dba_segments WHERE owner='HR' ORDER BY bytes DESC FETCH FIRST 20 ROWS ONLY;
■ SCHEMA OBJECTS — TABLES, VIEWS, SEQUENCES
TABLE DDL
CREATE TABLE employees ( emp_id NUMBER GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY, last_name VARCHAR2(100) NOT NULL, salary NUMBER(10,2) CHECK (salary > 0), hire_date DATE DEFAULT SYSDATE, dept_id NUMBER REFERENCES departments(dept_id) ON DELETE SET NULL); ALTER TABLE employees ADD (email VARCHAR2(200) UNIQUE); ALTER TABLE employees MODIFY (salary NUMBER(12,2)); ALTER TABLE employees DROP COLUMN email; ALTER TABLE employees RENAME COLUMN last_name TO surname; ALTER TABLE employees ENABLE CONSTRAINT pk_employees; ALTER TABLE employees DISABLE CONSTRAINT fk_dept_id; TRUNCATE TABLE employees; DROP TABLE employees PURGE; FLASHBACK TABLE employees TO BEFORE DROP;
VIEWS, SEQUENCES & SYNONYMS
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW emp_dept_v AS SELECT e.emp_id, e.last_name, d.dept_name FROM employees e JOIN departments d USING (dept_id); CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW emp_summary BUILD IMMEDIATE REFRESH FAST ON COMMIT AS SELECT dept_id, COUNT(*) cnt, AVG(salary) avg_sal FROM employees GROUP BY dept_id; CREATE SEQUENCE emp_seq START WITH 1000 INCREMENT BY 1 NOCACHE NOCYCLE; SELECT emp_seq.NEXTVAL FROM DUAL; CREATE SYNONYM emp FOR hr.employees; CREATE PUBLIC SYNONYM departments FOR hr.departments;
PL/SQL OBJECTS
CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE raise_salary( p_emp_id IN NUMBER, p_pct IN NUMBER) AS BEGIN UPDATE employees SET salary = salary*(1+p_pct/100) WHERE emp_id = p_emp_id; COMMIT; END;/ CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION get_sal(p_id NUMBER) RETURN NUMBER AS v_sal NUMBER; BEGIN SELECT salary INTO v_sal FROM employees WHERE emp_id=p_id; RETURN v_sal; END;/ CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg_emp_audit BEFORE UPDATE ON employees FOR EACH ROW BEGIN INSERT INTO audit_log VALUES(USER, SYSDATE, :OLD.salary, :NEW.salary); END;/
■ DATA OPERATIONS — DML & TRANSACTION CONTROL
INSERT / UPDATE / DELETE / MERGE
INSERT INTO employees (last_name, salary, dept_id) VALUES ('Smith', 65000, 10); INSERT INTO employees SELECT * FROM employees_bak WHERE dept_id=20; UPDATE employees SET salary = salary*1.10 WHERE dept_id = 10 AND ROWNUM <= 100; DELETE FROM employees WHERE emp_id = 999; -- MERGE (Oracle UPSERT ≈ SQL Server MERGE) MERGE INTO employees t USING (SELECT 100 id,'Jones' nm FROM DUAL) s ON (t.emp_id = s.id) WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET t.last_name = s.nm WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (emp_id, last_name) VALUES (s.id, s.nm);
TRANSACTIONS & FLASHBACK
COMMIT; ROLLBACK; SAVEPOINT sp_before_delete; ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT sp_before_delete; -- Flashback Query (point-in-time SELECT) SELECT * FROM employees AS OF TIMESTAMP (SYSTIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '1' HOUR) WHERE dept_id = 10; -- Flashback Table ALTER TABLE employees ENABLE ROW MOVEMENT; FLASHBACK TABLE employees TO TIMESTAMP (SYSTIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '30' MINUTE); -- Undo exploration SELECT operation, row_id, undo_sql FROM flashback_transaction_query WHERE xid = HEXTORAW('00050007000000A5');
ORACLE-SPECIFIC SELECT FEATURES
-- ROWNUM / ROW_NUMBER SELECT * FROM employees WHERE ROWNUM <= 10; SELECT * FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC FETCH FIRST 10 ROWS ONLY; -- 12c+ -- Analytic functions SELECT emp_id, salary, RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id ORDER BY salary DESC) rnk, LAG(salary,1) OVER (ORDER BY hire_date) prev_sal FROM employees; -- Hierarchical query SELECT LPAD(' ',LEVEL*2)||emp_id, mgr_id FROM employees START WITH mgr_id IS NULL CONNECT BY PRIOR emp_id = mgr_id; DUAL; -- Single-row dummy table SELECT SYSDATE, SYSTIMESTAMP, SYS_GUID() FROM DUAL;
■ PERFORMANCE MONITORING & TUNING
EXPLAIN PLAN & STATS
EXPLAIN PLAN FOR SELECT * FROM employees WHERE dept_id = 10; SELECT * FROM TABLE(DBMS_XPLAN.DISPLAY(format=>'ALL')); -- Auto trace (SQL*Plus) SET AUTOTRACE ON EXPLAIN STATISTICS; -- Gather statistics EXEC DBMS_STATS.GATHER_TABLE_STATS('HR','EMPLOYEES', cascade=>TRUE, estimate_percent=>DBMS_STATS.AUTO_SAMPLE_SIZE); EXEC DBMS_STATS.GATHER_SCHEMA_STATS('HR'); EXEC DBMS_STATS.GATHER_DATABASE_STATS(gather_sys=>TRUE); -- Lock/stale stats EXEC DBMS_STATS.LOCK_TABLE_STATS('HR','EMPLOYEES'); SELECT * FROM dba_tab_statistics WHERE stale_stats='YES';
AWR & ASH QUERIES
-- Create manual AWR snapshot EXEC DBMS_WORKLOAD_REPOSITORY.CREATE_SNAPSHOT(); -- List snapshots SELECT snap_id, begin_interval_time, end_interval_time FROM dba_hist_snapshot ORDER BY 1 DESC FETCH FIRST 10 ROWS ONLY; -- Top SQL from AWR SELECT sql_id, elapsed_time_total/1e6 tot_sec, executions_total execs FROM dba_hist_sqlstat ORDER BY elapsed_time_total DESC FETCH FIRST 10 ROWS ONLY; -- Active Session History (last 1 hour) SELECT sql_id, event, COUNT(*) samples FROM v$active_session_history WHERE sample_time > SYSDATE - 1/24 GROUP BY sql_id, event ORDER BY 3 DESC FETCH FIRST 20 ROWS ONLY;
WAIT EVENTS & LOCKS
-- Top wait events SELECT event, total_waits, time_waited/100 sec_waited, average_wait/100 avg_sec FROM v$system_event WHERE wait_class != 'Idle' ORDER BY time_waited DESC FETCH FIRST 15 ROWS ONLY; -- Blocking locks SELECT l1.sid "Blocker SID", s1.username "Blocker", l2.sid "Waiter SID", s2.username "Waiter" FROM v$lock l1 JOIN v$lock l2 ON (l1.id1=l2.id1 AND l1.block=1 AND l2.request>0) JOIN v$session s1 ON l1.sid=s1.sid JOIN v$session s2 ON l2.sid=s2.sid; -- Hit ratios SELECT name, value FROM v$sysstat WHERE name IN ('db block gets','consistent gets', 'physical reads','redo size');
■ RMAN — RECOVERY MANAGER
RMAN BACKUP COMMANDS
rman target / -- Connect local rman target sys/pass@ORCL BACKUP DATABASE PLUS ARCHIVELOG DELETE INPUT; BACKUP DATABASE FORMAT '/backup/%d_%T_%s_%p.bkp'; BACKUP INCREMENTAL LEVEL 0 DATABASE; BACKUP INCREMENTAL LEVEL 1 DATABASE; BACKUP ARCHIVELOG ALL DELETE INPUT; BACKUP CURRENT CONTROLFILE; BACKUP SPFILE; BACKUP TABLESPACE users; BACKUP DATAFILE 5, 6; CONFIGURE RETENTION POLICY TO RECOVERY WINDOW OF 7 DAYS; CONFIGURE CONTROLFILE AUTOBACKUP ON; CONFIGURE CHANNEL DEVICE TYPE DISK FORMAT '/backup/rman/%d_%T_%s_%p.bkp' PARALLELISM 4;
RMAN RESTORE & VALIDATE
RESTORE DATABASE; RECOVER DATABASE; RESTORE DATABASE UNTIL TIME "TO_DATE('2024-01-15','YYYY-MM-DD')"; RECOVER DATABASE UNTIL TIME "TO_DATE('2024-01-15','YYYY-MM-DD')"; ALTER DATABASE OPEN RESETLOGS; RESTORE TABLESPACE users; RECOVER TABLESPACE users; RESTORE DATAFILE 5; RECOVER DATAFILE 5; CROSSCHECK BACKUP; DELETE EXPIRED BACKUP; DELETE OBSOLETE; REPORT OBSOLETE; LIST BACKUP SUMMARY; LIST BACKUP OF DATABASE COMPLETED AFTER 'SYSDATE-7'; VALIDATE DATABASE; VALIDATE DATAFILE 1; RESTORE DATABASE VALIDATE; -- test only, no actual restore
■ DATA PUMP — EXPDP / IMPDP
EXPDP — EXPORT
-- Full database export expdp system/pass FULL=Y DIRECTORY=DATA_PUMP_DIR \ DUMPFILE=full_%d_%T.dmp LOGFILE=full_exp.log \ COMPRESSION=ALL PARALLEL=4 -- Schema export expdp system/pass SCHEMAS=HR,SALES \ DIRECTORY=DATA_PUMP_DIR DUMPFILE=schemas_%d.dmp -- Table-level export expdp hr/pass TABLES=employees,departments \ DIRECTORY=DATA_PUMP_DIR DUMPFILE=emp_dept.dmp -- Export with query filter expdp hr/pass TABLES=employees \ QUERY=employees:'"WHERE dept_id=10"' \ DIRECTORY=DATA_PUMP_DIR DUMPFILE=hr_dept10.dmp -- Transportable tablespace export expdp system/pass TRANSPORT_TABLESPACES=APP_DATA \ DIRECTORY=DATA_PUMP_DIR DUMPFILE=tts_app.dmp
IMPDP — IMPORT
-- Full import impdp system/pass FULL=Y DIRECTORY=DATA_PUMP_DIR \ DUMPFILE=full_%d.dmp LOGFILE=full_imp.log PARALLEL=4 -- Remap schema (HR → HRDEV) impdp system/pass SCHEMAS=HR \ REMAP_SCHEMA=HR:HRDEV \ DIRECTORY=DATA_PUMP_DIR DUMPFILE=hr.dmp -- Remap tablespace impdp system/pass FULL=Y \ REMAP_TABLESPACE=OLD_TBS:NEW_TBS \ DIRECTORY=DATA_PUMP_DIR DUMPFILE=full.dmp -- Network mode (no dump file needed) impdp system/pass NETWORK_LINK=SOURCEDB \ SCHEMAS=HR REMAP_SCHEMA=HR:HR_NEW -- Generate SQL DDL only (no import) impdp system/pass SQLFILE=hr_ddl.sql \ SCHEMAS=HR DIRECTORY=DATA_PUMP_DIR DUMPFILE=hr.dmp
RAC — SRVCTL & CRSCTL
srvctl status database -d ORCL srvctl start database -d ORCL srvctl stop instance -d ORCL -i ORCL1 srvctl add service -d ORCL -s mysvc \ -r ORCL1 -a ORCL2 -e SELECT -m BASIC srvctl start service -d ORCL -s mysvc crsctl status resource -t -- all resources crsctl check cluster crsctl query css votedisk olsnodes -n -- list nodes ocrcheck -- RAC SQL SELECT inst_id,instance_name,status FROM gv$instance; SELECT inst_id,sid,status,sql_id FROM gv$session;
DATA GUARD — DGMGRL
dgmgrl / "show configuration" dgmgrl / "show database verbose ORCL_STBY" dgmgrl / "switchover to 'orcl_stby'" dgmgrl / "failover to 'orcl_stby'" dgmgrl / "enable fast_start failover" dgmgrl / "validate database 'orcl_stby'" -- Primary setup ALTER SYSTEM SET LOG_ARCHIVE_DEST_2= 'SERVICE=standby ASYNC DB_UNIQUE_NAME=ORCL_STBY'; ALTER SYSTEM SET STANDBY_FILE_MANAGEMENT=AUTO; -- Standby apply ALTER DATABASE RECOVER MANAGED STANDBY DATABASE USING CURRENT LOGFILE DISCONNECT; SELECT name,value FROM v$dataguard_stats; SELECT database_role,open_mode FROM v$database;
MULTITENANT — CDB/PDB
SHOW CON_NAME; -- current container SELECT name,open_mode FROM v$pdbs; -- Create / open / close CREATE PLUGGABLE DATABASE pdb1 ADMIN USER pdbadmin IDENTIFIED BY pass FILE_NAME_CONVERT=('/seed/','/pdb1/'); ALTER PLUGGABLE DATABASE pdb1 OPEN; ALTER PLUGGABLE DATABASE pdb1 CLOSE IMMEDIATE; ALTER PLUGGABLE DATABASE ALL OPEN; ALTER PLUGGABLE DATABASE ALL SAVE STATE; -- Switch container ALTER SESSION SET CONTAINER = pdb1; ALTER SESSION SET CONTAINER = CDB$ROOT; -- Clone & unplug CREATE PLUGGABLE DATABASE pdb2 FROM pdb1; ALTER PLUGGABLE DATABASE pdb1 UNPLUG INTO '/tmp/pdb1.xml'; DROP PLUGGABLE DATABASE pdb1 INCLUDING DATAFILES;
■ ORACLE INSTANCE — MEMORY STRUCTURES (SGA)
BUFFER CACHE
PurposeCaches data blocks read from disk. Eliminates re-reads for "hot" data blocks.
SQL Server equivBuffer Pool (shared, single pool for all databases)
Key parameterDB_CACHE_SIZE (or DB_BLOCK_SIZE × DB_CACHE_SIZE)
MonitorV$BUFFER_POOL_STATISTICS · V$DB_CACHE_ADVICE
PoolsDEFAULT / KEEP / RECYCLE — different retention policies
SHARED POOL
PurposeCaches parsed SQL/PL/SQL (Library Cache) + data dictionary metadata (Row Cache)
SQL Server equivPlan Cache (procedure cache) + system catalog cache
Key parameterSHARED_POOL_SIZE
MonitorV$LIBRARYCACHE · V$ROWCACHE · V$SHARED_POOL_ADVICE
Hard parseFull parse + optimization; causes "latch: shared pool" waits when too frequent
REDO LOG BUFFER
PurposeCircular in-memory buffer holding redo change vectors before LGWR flushes to disk
SQL Server equivLog cache — portion of buffer pool holding log pages before writing to .ldf
Key parameterLOG_BUFFER (typically 1MB–128MB)
LGWR flushesOn COMMIT · 1/3 full · every 3 seconds · log switch · DBWn requests
MonitorV$SYSSTAT "redo buffer allocation retries" (should be near 0)
LARGE POOL & JAVA POOL
Large PoolUGA for shared server; RMAN backup buffers; parallel query buffers
Java PoolJava VM session state and code; only needed if using Java stored procedures
Streams PoolOracle Streams / GoldenGate capture/apply buffers
In-Memory AreaOracle Database In-Memory (12c+): columnar format of tables for analytics
MonitorV$SGAINFO · V$SGA · V$SGASTAT for all SGA components
PGA — PROGRAM GLOBAL AREA
PurposePrivate memory per server process: sort area, hash join area, bitmap merge area, session info
SQL Server equivWorker thread memory — each SPID gets stack + query workspace from Buffer Pool
Key parameterPGA_AGGREGATE_TARGET / PGA_AGGREGATE_LIMIT (hard cap)
MonitorV$PGASTAT · V$PROCESS · V$SQL_WORKAREA_HISTOGRAM
SpillIf PGA insufficient, sort/hash spills to TEMP tablespace (like SQL Server TempDB spill)
AUTOMATIC MEMORY MANAGEMENT
MEMORY_TARGETOracle manages both SGA+PGA automatically (AMM — requires /dev/shm on Linux)
MEMORY_MAX_TARGETAbsolute ceiling for total instance memory
ASMM modeSGA_TARGET set; Oracle auto-tunes SGA components; PGA_AGGREGATE_TARGET for PGA
Manual modeSet each: DB_CACHE_SIZE, SHARED_POOL_SIZE, LOG_BUFFER individually
SQL ServerSQL Server auto-manages buffer pool; no equivalent to Oracle's granular SGA control
■ ORACLE BACKGROUND PROCESSES
ProcessFull NameFunctionSQL Server Equiv
LGWRLog WriterWrites redo from Redo Log Buffer → Online Redo Log files. Fires on COMMIT, 1/3 full, 3-sec timeout, log switchLog Writer thread (part of SQL Server engine, writes .ldf)
DBWnDatabase WriterWrites dirty blocks from Buffer Cache → Datafiles. Fires at checkpoint, low cache space, timeout. Multiple writers (n=0..9,a..z)Lazy Writer + Checkpoint Writer threads
CKPTCheckpoint ProcessSignals DBWn to flush; updates file headers and control file with checkpoint SCN. Does NOT write data blocksCheckpoint thread (updates boot page, signals writers)
ARCnArchiverCopies full Online Redo Logs → Archive Log destination. Multiple archivers possibleLog backup agent — no direct equivalent; SQL Server does log backups via SQL Agent
SMONSystem MonitorInstance recovery at startup; coalesces free space in tablespaces; cleans up temp segmentsRecovery thread + Ghost Cleanup task
PMONProcess MonitorCleans up failed user process resources (locks, latches, resources); registers services with listenerResource Monitor (RESOURCE_MONITOR) thread
MMONManageability MonitorCollects AWR snapshots; computes ADDM findings; monitors thresholds; purges old statsSQL Server scheduled collection jobs (none built-in directly)
MMANMemory ManagerImplements Automatic Shared Memory Management (ASMM); dynamically resizes SGA componentsNo equivalent; SQL Server has fixed Buffer Pool managed by Memory Manager internally
RECORecovererResolves in-doubt distributed transactions (two-phase commit failures)DTC (Distributed Transaction Coordinator) handles similar role
LGWRn / LMS / LMDRAC-specificLMSn = GCS (ships blocks between nodes); LMD = GES (lock management); LMON = cluster monitorNo equivalent — SQL Server FCI uses Windows Cluster; no Cache Fusion concept
■ ORACLE DATABASE — PHYSICAL STORAGE COMPONENTS
DATAFILES (.dbf)
WhatPhysical OS files storing all permanent data (tables, indexes, etc.). One or many per tablespace
SQL Server equiv.mdf (primary data file) and .ndf (secondary data files)
ViewV$DATAFILE · DBA_DATA_FILES · DBA_SEGMENTS
ContentDivided into Oracle blocks (2K–32K; default 8K). Block = smallest I/O unit
ONLINE REDO LOG FILES
WhatCircular log groups recording ALL changes. LGWR writes here. Required for crash recovery
SQL Server equiv.ldf (transaction log file) — but Oracle redo is circular; SQL Server grows until backup
Groups/MembersMinimum 2 groups; each group should have 2+ members (multiplexed) on different disks
ViewV$LOG · V$LOGFILE · V$LOG_HISTORY
Add groupALTER DATABASE ADD LOGFILE GROUP 4 ('/u02/redo4.log') SIZE 200M;
CONTROL FILE
WhatBinary file tracking DB name, datafile list, redo log list, current SCN, checkpoint info, RMAN catalog
SQL Server equivSystem catalog + boot page of master database (no single equivalent file)
MultiplexingAlways maintain 2–3 copies in CONTROL_FILES parameter. Loss = DB won't open
ViewV$CONTROLFILE · V$DATABASE · V$DATAFILE (control file content)
ARCHIVE LOG FILES
WhatCompleted online redo logs saved by ARCn. Required for PITR, Data Guard shipping, RMAN
SQL Server equivTransaction log backups — same purpose but Oracle keeps all logs until manually purged
EnableALTER DATABASE ARCHIVELOG; (MOUNT mode required)
ViewV$ARCHIVED_LOG · V$ARCHIVE_DEST · ARCHIVE LOG LIST (SQL*Plus)
SPFILE / INIT.ORA
SPFILEBinary server-side parameter file. Supports ALTER SYSTEM … SCOPE=SPFILE (persistent changes)
PFILEText init.ora. Editable manually. Loaded with STARTUP PFILE=...
SQL Server equivRegistry settings + sp_configure; SQL Server has no direct SPFILE equivalent
Location$ORACLE_HOME/dbs/spfile${ORACLE_SID}.ora (default)
ViewV$PARAMETER · V$SPPARAMETER · SHOW PARAMETER <name>
ASM — AUTOMATIC STORAGE MANAGEMENT
WhatOracle built-in volume manager + file system. Manages disk groups with automatic striping + mirroring
SQL Server equivWindows Storage Spaces / LVM + file system (no direct equivalent)
RedundancyEXTERNAL (none) / NORMAL (2-way mirror) / HIGH (3-way mirror)
ViewV$ASM_DISKGROUP · V$ASM_DISK · V$ASM_FILE
asmcmdShell for ASM: ls / cd / mkdir / rm / cp / du commands within ASM
■ ORACLE ↔ SQL SERVER COMPLETE MAPPING
SQL Server ConceptOracle EquivalentKey Difference
Instance (1 process, multi-thread)Instance (multi-process: LGWR, DBWn, CKPT...)Oracle uses separate OS processes per background function; SQL Server uses threads
Database (.mdf / .ldf / .ndf)Database (datafiles + redo logs + control file)Oracle separates redo (circular) from data; SQL Server .ldf is linear (grows until backup)
Schema (owned by user)Schema = User (same concept in Oracle)In Oracle, every user IS a schema. Objects belong to a schema = user namespace
TempDB (single shared DB)Temporary Tablespace (per-user or global)Oracle TEMP is a tablespace; SQL Server TempDB is a full database instance
Buffer PoolBuffer Cache (SGA component)Oracle Buffer Cache can have KEEP/RECYCLE sub-pools; SQL Server is one pool
Plan Cache / Procedure CacheLibrary Cache (in Shared Pool)Oracle stores cursor (parsed SQL) in Library Cache; reuse requires identical SQL text
Transaction Log (.ldf)Online Redo Log + Archive LogOracle redo is circular (fixed size groups); SQL Server log grows until log backup
CheckpointCheckpoint (CKPT signals DBWn)Oracle checkpoint is incremental by default; SQL Server has full + indirect checkpoints
FilegroupsTablespacesTablespaces are more granular; can be taken offline independently; transportable
Clustered IndexIndex-Organized Table (IOT)Oracle heap tables have no physical order; IOT stores rows in B-tree by PK
Non-Clustered IndexB-Tree Index (default)Oracle B-tree index stores ROWID; SQL Server non-clustered stores row locator (key or RID)
SQL Server AgentDBMS_SCHEDULER (built-in job scheduler)DBMS_SCHEDULER has programs, schedules, chains, windows; more granular than SQL Agent
Always On AGOracle Data GuardData Guard supports standby read queries (Active DG); AG requires Enterprise license too
Failover Cluster Instance (FCI)RAC (Real Application Clusters)RAC runs all nodes simultaneously (active-active); FCI is active-passive shared storage
SSMS GUISQL Developer / Enterprise Manager / SQL*PlusOracle has multiple tools; SQL Developer is closest GUI equivalent to SSMS
BCP / SSISData Pump (expdp/impdp) + SQL*LoaderData Pump is server-side (faster); SQL*Loader ≈ BULK INSERT; no direct SSIS equivalent
Linked ServersDatabase Links (DB Links)CREATE DATABASE LINK uses Oracle Net; transparent SELECT across databases
DMVs (sys.dm_*)V$ Dynamic Views + DBA_* Static ViewsV$ views show current runtime state; DBA_* show dictionary data; GV$ for RAC (global)
SQL Server Profiler / XEventsSQL Trace / TKPROF / Event 10046 / AWR / ASHOracle traces into a raw trace file; TKPROF formats it; ASH samples every second
Resource GovernorOracle Resource Manager (DBRM)Resource Manager has consumer groups, plans, CPU directives; more feature-rich
Transparent Data EncryptionOracle TDE (Tablespace or Column level)Oracle TDE can encrypt individual columns or full tablespaces; similar to SQL Server TDE
Contained DatabasePluggable Database (PDB)PDB is fully portable; can be unplugged/plugged between CDB containers
SQL Server AuditOracle Unified Auditing (12c+)Unified Auditing captures to AUDSYS.AUD$UNIFIED; richer policy-based control
SQL Server ReplicationOracle GoldenGate / StreamsGoldenGate is heterogeneous; works across databases/platforms; Streams is deprecated
IDENTITY columnSEQUENCE + DEFAULT / GENERATED AS IDENTITY (12c+)12c+ supports GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY; earlier used sequence.NEXTVAL
TOP N / LIMITROWNUM (legacy) / FETCH FIRST N ROWS ONLY (12c+)FETCH FIRST syntax is standard SQL; ROWNUM applies before ORDER BY in subqueries
△ ORACLE INDEX ARCHITECTURE — OVERVIEW
Oracle indexes store a sorted copy of one or more column values alongside the ROWID (physical address: file#, block#, row#) of each row. Unlike SQL Server, Oracle uses heap tables by default — rows have no physical order unless you use an Index-Organized Table (IOT). The query optimizer automatically chooses indexes based on selectivity, statistics, and cost. Oracle supports many index types for different access patterns.
△ B-TREE INDEX — DEFAULT & MOST COMMON
WHAT & WHY
StructureBalanced tree: root → branch blocks → leaf blocks. Leaf blocks contain (key value, ROWID) pairs, doubly linked for range scans
Best forHigh-cardinality columns (unique or near-unique): emp_id, email, order#, SSN
OperationsEquality (=), range (<, >, BETWEEN), ORDER BY, MIN/MAX, unique lookups
Avoid whenLow cardinality (gender, status) — full table scan often faster for >5% of rows
SQL Server equivNon-Clustered Index — same B-tree structure, same use cases
NULL behaviorOracle B-tree does NOT index NULL values (unlike SQL Server). Use IS NOT NULL in WHERE to use index
SYNTAX
-- Basic B-Tree CREATE INDEX idx_emp_lname ON employees(last_name); -- Composite (multi-column) — leftmost prefix rule CREATE INDEX idx_emp_dept_sal ON employees(dept_id, salary); -- Descending key CREATE INDEX idx_emp_sal_desc ON employees(salary DESC); -- With COMPRESS (removes repeated leading key values) CREATE INDEX idx_ord_cust ON orders(customer_id, order_date) COMPRESS 1; -- Invisible (optimizer ignores; still maintained) CREATE INDEX idx_emp_email ON employees(email) INVISIBLE; -- Monitor & Rebuild ALTER INDEX idx_emp_lname MONITORING USAGE; ALTER INDEX idx_emp_lname REBUILD ONLINE; SELECT * FROM v$object_usage WHERE index_name='IDX_EMP_LNAME';
B-TREE ACCESS TYPES
INDEX UNIQUE SCANSingle leaf block lookup. Unique key = equality. Most efficient
INDEX RANGE SCANTraverses leaf blocks in order for range predicates (<, >, BETWEEN, LIKE 'A%')
INDEX FAST FULL SCANMulti-block I/O of all leaf blocks. Faster than full table scan for index-only queries. Ignores order
INDEX FULL SCANReads all leaf blocks in order (avoids SORT). Used when ORDER BY matches index
INDEX SKIP SCANComposite index used without leading column in WHERE. Optimizer "skips" through distinct values of leading column
INDEX JOINMultiple indexes on same table are hash-joined to avoid table access. Query only needs indexed columns
△ UNIQUE INDEX
UNIQUE INDEX
PurposeEnforces uniqueness constraint at the storage engine level. Oracle creates one automatically for PRIMARY KEY and UNIQUE constraints
vs UNIQUE constraintYou can create a unique index separately from the constraint — allows DEFERRABLE checks
NULL behaviorMultiple NULLs allowed in a UNIQUE index (unlike SQL Server which allows only one NULL)
SQL Server equivCREATE UNIQUE INDEX / UNIQUE constraint — same behavior except NULL handling
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX uk_emp_email ON employees(email); -- Unique with NOT NULL enforced: ALTER TABLE employees ADD CONSTRAINT uk_emp_ssn UNIQUE (ssn) USING INDEX (CREATE UNIQUE INDEX uk_ssn ON employees(ssn)); -- Partial unique index (12c+ expression): CREATE UNIQUE INDEX uk_active_emp ON employees(CASE WHEN status='ACTIVE' THEN emp_id END);
COMPOSITE INDEX — LEADING COLUMN RULE
RuleOracle can use a composite index only if the WHERE clause includes the LEADING column(s)
ExampleINDEX(dept_id, job_id, salary): usable with dept_id alone, dept_id+job_id, or all 3. NOT usable with job_id alone
Skip scanException: INDEX SKIP SCAN can skip the leading column if it has low cardinality and skipping is cheaper than full scan
Column orderPut most selective (high cardinality) columns first for equality; range column last
SQL Server equivSame composite index leading column rule applies to SQL Server non-clustered indexes
CREATE INDEX idx_emp_dept_job_sal ON employees(dept_id, job_id, salary); -- These use the index: WHERE dept_id = 10 WHERE dept_id = 10 AND job_id = 'MGR' WHERE dept_id = 10 AND salary > 50000 -- This does NOT use it (no leading column): WHERE job_id = 'MGR' -- full scan
△ BITMAP INDEX
BITMAP INDEX — HOW IT WORKS
StructureFor each distinct key value, stores a compressed BIT ARRAY marking which rows have that value (1=yes, 0=no)
Best forLOW cardinality columns: status ('ACTIVE','INACTIVE'), gender (M/F), region (N/S/E/W), boolean flags
AND/ORMultiple bitmap indexes can be combined with bitwise AND/OR operations very efficiently (no random I/O)
OLAP/DW useDesigned for data warehouse / reporting queries with many WHERE conditions on dimension columns
DO NOT use inOLTP environments. Bitmap index causes table-level locking on DML (UPDATE on one row locks all rows with same key value)
SQL Server equivNo direct equivalent. SQL Server uses standard B-tree even for low cardinality. Columnstore index is closest for DW
CREATE BITMAP INDEX bix_emp_status ON employees(status); CREATE BITMAP INDEX bix_emp_gender ON employees(gender); -- Oracle can combine both bitmaps in one query: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM employees WHERE status='ACTIVE' AND gender='F'; -- Uses: BITMAP AND of bix_emp_status + bix_emp_gender -- Very efficient for COUNT(*) — no table access needed!
FUNCTION-BASED INDEX
WhatIndex on the RESULT of a function or expression applied to a column
Best forCase-insensitive search (UPPER/LOWER), date truncation, derived calculations
RequirementQuery's WHERE must use the EXACT same function expression for the index to be used
SQL Server equivComputed column index — SQL Server indexes computed columns; Oracle can index expressions directly
-- Case-insensitive last name search: CREATE INDEX idx_emp_upper_lname ON employees(UPPER(last_name)); -- To use it, query must match: SELECT * FROM employees WHERE UPPER(last_name) = 'SMITH'; -- uses index -- Date truncation (find all rows for a given day): CREATE INDEX idx_sales_trunc_dt ON sales(TRUNC(sale_date)); SELECT * FROM sales WHERE TRUNC(sale_date) = DATE '2024-01-15'; -- Expression index (virtual column alternative): CREATE INDEX idx_emp_monthly ON employees(salary / 12);
△ INDEX-ORGANIZED TABLE, REVERSE KEY & INVISIBLE INDEX
INDEX-ORGANIZED TABLE (IOT)
WhatEntire table stored in B-tree order by primary key. No separate heap segment. Row = leaf block entry
SQL Server equivClustered Index — SQL Server tables ARE IOTs when a clustered index exists
Best forLookup tables, code tables, reference data — frequently accessed by PK with no scattered heap reads
DisadvantageRow migration if rows grow; secondary indexes include logical ROWID (slower than physical ROWID)
CREATE TABLE postal_codes ( zip_code CHAR(5) PRIMARY KEY, city VARCHAR2(50), state CHAR(2), latitude NUMBER, longitude NUMBER ) ORGANIZATION INDEX PCTTHRESHOLD 20 INCLUDING state OVERFLOW TABLESPACE overflow_tbs; -- Row stored IN the B-tree by zip_code PK -- Non-PK columns after PCTTHRESHOLD spill to overflow
REVERSE KEY INDEX
WhatBytes of each key value are reversed before insertion. 1001→1001 becomes 1001→1001 reversed byte-by-byte
Problem solvedIn RAC: sequential keys (sequences, timestamps) always insert into rightmost leaf block → "right-hand index contention." Reversal spreads inserts across all leaf blocks
LimitationCannot support range scans (BETWEEN, <, >) because key values are scrambled
SQL Server equivNo direct equivalent; SQL Server uses fill factor to reduce page splits
CREATE INDEX idx_emp_id_rev ON employees(emp_id) REVERSE; -- Good for: WHERE emp_id = 12345 (equality only) -- BAD for: WHERE emp_id BETWEEN 10000 AND 20000 -- Alternative: use HASH PARTITION on the index
INVISIBLE & PARTIAL INDEXES
InvisibleIndex is maintained but optimizer ignores it. Use to test impact before enabling. ALTER INDEX … INVISIBLE / VISIBLE
Force useALTER SESSION SET OPTIMIZER_USE_INVISIBLE_INDEXES=TRUE to test invisible index
Partial (12c+)Index only a SUBSET of rows. Use INDEXING ON/OFF clause on partitions to exclude cold data from index
-- Invisible: safe A/B testing CREATE INDEX idx_new_feature ON t(col1) INVISIBLE; ALTER SESSION SET OPTIMIZER_USE_INVISIBLE_INDEXES=TRUE; EXPLAIN PLAN FOR SELECT ...; -- test plan with index ALTER INDEX idx_new_feature VISIBLE; -- promote -- Partial index on partitioned table: CREATE TABLE sales (...) PARTITION BY RANGE(sale_date) (PARTITION p_archive ... INDEXING OFF, -- exclude old data PARTITION p_current ... INDEXING ON); -- index current only CREATE INDEX idx_sales_cust ON sales(cust_id) INDEXING PARTIAL; -- only indexes INDEXING ON partitions
△ INDEX SELECTION GUIDE
ScenarioRecommended IndexReason
Primary key or unique column lookupB-Tree UNIQUEHigh cardinality, equality, created automatically with constraint
Foreign key join columnB-Tree (non-unique)Prevents lock propagation; speeds joins; often missing and causes performance issues
LIKE 'ABC%' prefix searchB-TreeRange scan on prefix. LIKE '%ABC%' (infix) still requires full table scan
WHERE UPPER(name) = 'SMITH'Function-Based Index on UPPER(name)Without FBI, function prevents B-tree use. Query must match exact expression
Low cardinality column (status, gender, region) in DW/reportingBitmap IndexVery efficient for AND/OR combinations on low-cardinality dims; never in OLTP
Table always accessed by primary key, small rowsIndex-Organized Table (IOT)Eliminates heap access; row is in the index. No wasted ROWID fetch
Sequential inserts causing RAC right-hand contentionReverse Key IndexSpreads sequential key inserts across blocks. Sacrifices range scan capability
Hot index with many repeated leading key valuesB-Tree with COMPRESS NKey compression removes duplicate leading key storage; reduces index size & I/O
Testing new index without affecting production optimizerInvisible IndexMaintained but hidden from optimizer until explicitly enabled per session
Large partitioned table; only recent partitions queriedPartial Index (INDEXING ON/OFF)Index only "hot" partitions; reduces index size; archived partitions excluded
SELECT using only indexed columns (no heap fetch needed)Any index (INDEX-ONLY SCAN)Oracle can satisfy query from index alone if all columns are in the index (covering)
Many columns queried with OR conditions in DW contextMultiple Bitmap IndexesOracle performs BITMAP AND/OR operations between multiple bitmap indexes — extremely fast for COUNT(*) analytics
△ INDEX MAINTENANCE COMMANDS
REBUILD & COALESCE
-- Rebuild (drops and recreates the index — frees space) ALTER INDEX idx_emp_lname REBUILD; ALTER INDEX idx_emp_lname REBUILD ONLINE; -- no DML blocking ALTER INDEX idx_emp_lname REBUILD TABLESPACE idx_tbs; ALTER INDEX idx_emp_lname REBUILD COMPUTE STATISTICS; -- Coalesce (merges leaf blocks without rebuild — faster, less space) ALTER INDEX idx_emp_lname COALESCE; -- Shrink (12c+ — reclaims space, minimal locks) ALTER INDEX idx_emp_lname SHRINK SPACE; -- Validate structure (check for corruption) ANALYZE INDEX idx_emp_lname VALIDATE STRUCTURE; SELECT name, lf_rows, del_lf_rows, del_lf_rows/lf_rows*100 pct_deleted FROM index_stats; -- after VALIDATE STRUCTURE -- Check if rebuild needed (del_lf_rows > 20% of lf_rows usually means rebuild)
INDEX MONITORING & VIEWS
-- Check index status and visibility SELECT index_name, index_type, uniqueness, status, visibility, compression FROM dba_indexes WHERE table_name='EMPLOYEES'; -- Find unused indexes (monitor usage) ALTER INDEX idx_emp_lname MONITORING USAGE; -- ... wait some time / workload ... ALTER INDEX idx_emp_lname NOMONITORING USAGE; SELECT index_name, used FROM v$object_usage WHERE index_name='IDX_EMP_LNAME'; -- Find invalid indexes (after DDL like MOVE TABLE) SELECT owner, index_name, status FROM dba_indexes WHERE status = 'UNUSABLE'; -- Index fragmentation check SELECT name, HEIGHT, LF_ROWS, LF_BLKS, BR_ROWS, BR_BLKS, DEL_LF_ROWS FROM index_stats; -- populated by ANALYZE INDEX ... VALIDATE
■ ORACLE PARTITIONING — OVERVIEW
Oracle Partitioning divides a large table or index into smaller, manageable segments while remaining transparent to SQL queries. Each partition is stored independently but queried as one logical table. Key benefits: Partition Pruning (optimizer scans only relevant partitions), Partition-wise Joins (parallel join per partition pair), Partition Maintenance (ADD/DROP/EXCHANGE partition — instant DDL), and ILM (Information Lifecycle Management) (move old partitions to cheaper storage). SQL Server equivalent is Table Partitioning (similar concept, less flexible strategies).
■ 1. RANGE PARTITIONING — MOST COMMON
HOW IT WORKS
Partition keySingle column or multiple columns define the range boundary. Most common: DATE or TIMESTAMP columns
VALUES LESS THANEach partition holds rows where key < the boundary value. MAXVALUE = catch-all last partition
PruningWHERE sale_date = '2024-03-01' → optimizer scans ONLY the matching partition — can eliminate 99%+ of I/O
Best forTime-series data: orders, logs, events, financial records — queried by date range
SQL Server equivTable partitioning with partition function / scheme — same concept, Oracle has more strategies
CREATE TABLE sales ( sale_id NUMBER, sale_date DATE NOT NULL, customer_id NUMBER, amount NUMBER ) PARTITION BY RANGE (sale_date) ( PARTITION p_2022 VALUES LESS THAN (DATE '2023-01-01'), PARTITION p_2023 VALUES LESS THAN (DATE '2024-01-01'), PARTITION p_2024 VALUES LESS THAN (DATE '2025-01-01'), PARTITION p_future VALUES LESS THAN (MAXVALUE) );
INTERVAL PARTITIONING (11g+) — AUTO-CREATE
WhatExtension of RANGE. Oracle automatically creates new partitions when data arrives beyond current boundaries. Zero manual partition management
Best forTables growing with time where you don't want to manually add partitions each month/year
SQL Server equivNo direct equivalent; SQL Server requires manually adding partitions or using a custom script
CREATE TABLE orders ( order_id NUMBER, order_date DATE NOT NULL, total NUMBER ) PARTITION BY RANGE (order_date) INTERVAL (NUMTOYMINTERVAL(1,'MONTH')) -- auto monthly (PARTITION p_initial VALUES LESS THAN (DATE '2024-01-01')); -- Oracle auto-creates: p_2024_01, p_2024_02, etc. -- as new rows arrive in each month -- Interval by year: INTERVAL (NUMTOYMINTERVAL(1,'YEAR')) -- Interval by week: INTERVAL (NUMTODSINTERVAL(7,'DAY'))
■ 2. LIST PARTITIONING — DISCRETE VALUES
HOW IT WORKS
Partition keyRows are assigned to partitions based on DISCRETE values of the partition key (no ranges)
VALUES ()Each partition declares the list of values it handles. DEFAULT catches anything not listed
Best forRegion (NORTH/SOUTH/EAST/WEST), status codes, country codes, business unit, product category
Multi-column (19c+)Partitioning on multiple columns simultaneously: PARTITION BY LIST (region, product_line)
CREATE TABLE customers ( cust_id NUMBER, region VARCHAR2(10), name VARCHAR2(100) ) PARTITION BY LIST (region) ( PARTITION p_north VALUES ('NORTH', 'NE', 'NW'), PARTITION p_south VALUES ('SOUTH', 'SE', 'SW'), PARTITION p_east VALUES ('EAST'), PARTITION p_west VALUES ('WEST'), PARTITION p_other VALUES (DEFAULT) ); -- Query pruning: WHERE region='EAST' scans only p_east
AUTOMATIC LIST PARTITIONING (12.2+)
WhatA new partition is automatically created for any new distinct value not yet represented
Best forTables partitioned by country code, store ID, or any key where new distinct values are added over time
CREATE TABLE store_sales ( sale_id NUMBER, store_id VARCHAR2(10), amount NUMBER ) PARTITION BY LIST (store_id) AUTOMATIC (PARTITION p_initial VALUES ('STORE_001')); -- When STORE_002 arrives for first time: -- Oracle auto-creates PARTITION SYS_P123 for STORE_002
■ 3. HASH PARTITIONING — EVEN DISTRIBUTION
HOW IT WORKS
AlgorithmOracle applies an internal hash function to the partition key. Rows are evenly distributed across N partitions
No pruningEquality predicates allow pruning to ONE partition. Range/inequality queries scan ALL partitions
Best forEliminating partition I/O contention (hotspot); parallelizing joins; even storage distribution across diskgroups
Partition countUse powers of 2 (2, 4, 8, 16, 32...) for best distribution guarantee
SQL Server equivNo direct equivalent; filegroups can be used but SQL Server has no hash partitioning strategy
CREATE TABLE large_fact ( fact_id NUMBER PRIMARY KEY, product_id NUMBER, amount NUMBER ) PARTITION BY HASH (fact_id) PARTITIONS 16 STORE IN (tbs1, tbs2, tbs3, tbs4); -- Evenly spread across 4 tablespaces, 16 partitions -- WHERE fact_id = 12345 → 1 partition scanned -- WHERE amount > 100 → all 16 partitions scanned
PARTITION-WISE JOINS
WhatWhen two tables are hash-partitioned on the same join key, Oracle joins matching partition pairs in parallel — eliminates full table shuffling
Full PW JoinBoth tables identically partitioned on join key → n parallel join operations, each handling 1/n of data
Partial PW JoinOnly one table partitioned → smaller table can be broadcast to each partition worker
BenefitMassive parallel speedup for DW star schema joins: fact ⋈ dimension on same hash key
-- Both partitioned by customer_id on 8 partitions: CREATE TABLE orders (...) PARTITION BY HASH(cust_id) PARTITIONS 8; CREATE TABLE returns (...) PARTITION BY HASH(cust_id) PARTITIONS 8; -- This join benefits from FULL partition-wise join: SELECT o.order_id, r.return_id FROM orders o JOIN returns r USING (cust_id) WHERE o.order_date > DATE '2024-01-01'; -- Plan shows: PARTITION HASH ALL + HASH JOIN (PARTITION-WISE)
■ 4. COMPOSITE PARTITIONING — TWO-LEVEL STRATEGIES
RANGE-HASH (MOST POPULAR COMPOSITE)
Level 1Range partitioning on DATE — for time-based pruning and lifecycle management
Level 2Hash sub-partitioning on a high-cardinality key — for even distribution and parallelism within each date range
Best forVery large fact tables: time-based partitions for ILM + hash sub-partitions to avoid hot blocks
CREATE TABLE big_sales ( sale_id NUMBER, sale_date DATE, product_id NUMBER, amount NUMBER ) PARTITION BY RANGE (sale_date) SUBPARTITION BY HASH (product_id) SUBPARTITIONS 8 (PARTITION p_2023 VALUES LESS THAN (DATE '2024-01-01'), PARTITION p_2024 VALUES LESS THAN (DATE '2025-01-01') ); -- 2 range partitions × 8 hash sub-partitions = 16 total segments -- WHERE sale_date='2024-03-01' AND product_id=999 → 1 sub-partition
RANGE-LIST, LIST-RANGE, LIST-HASH
-- RANGE-LIST: Date range, then list sub-partition by region CREATE TABLE sales_rl (...) PARTITION BY RANGE (sale_date) SUBPARTITION BY LIST (region) SUBPARTITION TEMPLATE ( SUBPARTITION p_north VALUES ('NORTH'), SUBPARTITION p_south VALUES ('SOUTH'), SUBPARTITION p_other VALUES (DEFAULT) ) (PARTITION p_2024 VALUES LESS THAN (DATE '2025-01-01'), PARTITION p_2025 VALUES LESS THAN (DATE '2026-01-01')); -- LIST-HASH: Region first, hash within each region CREATE TABLE sales_lh (...) PARTITION BY LIST (region) SUBPARTITION BY HASH (cust_id) SUBPARTITIONS 4 (PARTITION p_north VALUES ('NORTH'), PARTITION p_south VALUES ('SOUTH')); -- LIST-LIST: Two discrete dimensions (12.2+) CREATE TABLE inventory (...) PARTITION BY LIST (warehouse_id) SUBPARTITION BY LIST (product_type) ...;
■ 5. REFERENCE PARTITIONING (11g+)
HOW IT WORKS
WhatChild table is partitioned IDENTICALLY to its parent table via a foreign key relationship. No partition key column needed in child
WhyEnables full partition-wise joins between parent and child without duplicating the partition key in the child
CascadeAdding/dropping a partition on the parent automatically applies to the child
SQL Server equivNo equivalent — SQL Server requires matching partition functions manually on related tables
-- Parent table: partitioned by order_date CREATE TABLE orders ( order_id NUMBER PRIMARY KEY, order_date DATE, cust_id NUMBER ) PARTITION BY RANGE (order_date) ( ... ); -- Child table: inherits same partitioning via FK CREATE TABLE order_lines ( line_id NUMBER, order_id NUMBER NOT NULL REFERENCES orders(order_id), -- FK to parent product_id NUMBER, qty NUMBER ) PARTITION BY REFERENCE (fk_order_lines_orders); -- order_lines is automatically partitioned same as orders -- No order_date column needed in order_lines!
PARTITION MAINTENANCE OPERATIONS
-- Add new partition ALTER TABLE sales ADD PARTITION p_2026 VALUES LESS THAN (DATE '2027-01-01'); -- Drop a partition (DELETE rows + segment) ALTER TABLE sales DROP PARTITION p_2020; -- Truncate partition (fast DELETE — keep structure) ALTER TABLE sales TRUNCATE PARTITION p_2020; -- Move partition (to different tablespace) ALTER TABLE sales MOVE PARTITION p_2020 TABLESPACE archive_tbs; -- Exchange partition (swap with standalone table) -- p_2020 ↔ stage_table (instant, just redefine segments) ALTER TABLE sales EXCHANGE PARTITION p_2020 WITH TABLE stage_table INCLUDING INDEXES; -- Split partition (into two) ALTER TABLE sales SPLIT PARTITION p_future AT (DATE '2025-07-01') INTO (PARTITION p_2025h1, PARTITION p_2025h2); -- Merge two partitions into one ALTER TABLE sales MERGE PARTITIONS p_2020, p_2021 INTO PARTITION p_old;
■ LOCAL vs GLOBAL INDEXES ON PARTITIONED TABLES
LOCAL INDEX — PARTITION-ALIGNED
WhatOne index segment PER table partition. Index is automatically partitioned the same way as the table
BenefitDROP PARTITION or TRUNCATE PARTITION only marks the corresponding index partition UNUSABLE — rest stay valid
UsabilityOnly guaranteed unique if partition key is included in the index key (otherwise local non-unique)
Best forQueries with partition key in WHERE clause; partition maintenance operations; lifecycle management
-- Local B-Tree index (auto-partitioned with table): CREATE INDEX idx_sales_cust_local ON sales(customer_id) LOCAL; -- Local prefixed (partition key is LEADING column): CREATE INDEX idx_sales_date_amt ON sales(sale_date, amount) LOCAL; -- partition key = sale_date -- Local non-prefixed (partition key NOT leading): CREATE INDEX idx_sales_prod_local ON sales(product_id) LOCAL; -- sale_date is NOT in index -- Rebuild one partition's index only: ALTER INDEX idx_sales_cust_local REBUILD PARTITION p_2024;
GLOBAL INDEX — SPANS ALL PARTITIONS
WhatSingle index structure spanning all table partitions. Can be non-partitioned or partitioned differently from the table
UniquenessOnly a GLOBAL index can enforce uniqueness across all partitions without requiring the partition key in the index
DisadvantageDROP/TRUNCATE/MOVE partition marks the ENTIRE global index as UNUSABLE (must rebuild all)
UPDATE INDEXESALTER TABLE ... DROP PARTITION ... UPDATE INDEXES maintains global indexes during DDL (slower but no downtime)
Best forUnique enforcement on non-partition-key; queries that don't filter by partition key
-- Global non-partitioned (one index, all partitions): CREATE UNIQUE INDEX uk_sales_id ON sales(sale_id); -- GLOBAL by default (no LOCAL keyword) -- Global partitioned index (index partitioned differently): CREATE INDEX idx_sales_product_global ON sales(product_id) GLOBAL PARTITION BY RANGE (product_id) ( PARTITION idx_p1 VALUES LESS THAN (1000), PARTITION idx_p2 VALUES LESS THAN (MAXVALUE)); -- Maintain global indexes during partition DDL: ALTER TABLE sales DROP PARTITION p_2019 UPDATE GLOBAL INDEXES; -- rebuilds global index online
■ PARTITION PRUNING & MONITORING
PARTITION PRUNING
WhatOptimizer eliminates irrelevant partitions at parse time (static pruning) or at runtime (dynamic pruning). Only qualifying partitions are accessed
Static pruningPartition key in WHERE with literal value: WHERE sale_date = DATE '2024-03-01' — 1 partition accessed
Dynamic pruningPartition key compared to a bind variable — Oracle determines partition at execute time
Check in planEXPLAIN PLAN shows PARTITION RANGE SINGLE / PARTITION RANGE ITERATOR / PARTITION RANGE ALL
Subquery pruningWHERE dept_id IN (SELECT dept_id FROM dept WHERE region='WEST') — pruned if dept is also partitioned
-- Check partitioning in explain plan: EXPLAIN PLAN FOR SELECT * FROM sales WHERE sale_date = DATE '2024-03-15'; SELECT * FROM TABLE(DBMS_XPLAN.DISPLAY()); -- Look for: Pstart=3 Pstop=3 (single partition #3) -- Or: PARTITION RANGE SINGLE — one partition accessed -- Bad pruning (no partition key in WHERE): SELECT * FROM sales WHERE product_id = 100; -- Shows: PARTITION RANGE ALL — all partitions scanned
PARTITION MONITORING VIEWS
-- List all partitions of a table SELECT partition_name, partition_position, high_value, num_rows, blocks, last_analyzed FROM dba_tab_partitions WHERE table_name = 'SALES' AND owner = 'HR' ORDER BY partition_position; -- Sub-partitions SELECT subpartition_name, partition_name, num_rows, blocks FROM dba_tab_subpartitions WHERE table_name = 'BIG_SALES'; -- Size per partition SELECT p.partition_name, s.bytes/1024/1024 "SIZE_MB" FROM dba_tab_partitions p JOIN dba_segments s ON s.partition_name = p.partition_name AND s.segment_name = p.table_name WHERE p.table_name = 'SALES' ORDER BY p.partition_position; -- Stale stats on partitions SELECT partition_name, stale_stats, last_analyzed FROM dba_tab_statistics WHERE table_name = 'SALES' AND object_type='PARTITION' ORDER BY partition_name;
PARTITIONING QUICK GUIDE
StrategyBest Use CasePruning
RANGETime-series (dates, timestamps, SCN)Range & equality on partition key
INTERVALAuto-growing range (monthly, yearly)Same as RANGE; auto partition creation
LISTDiscrete categories (region, status, country)Equality on partition key values
AUTO LISTGrowing set of distinct valuesSame as LIST; auto partition creation
HASHEven distribution, hot-spot eliminationEquality only (to 1 partition)
RANGE-HASHTime + distribution (large fact tables)Best of range+hash pruning
RANGE-LISTTime + discrete dimensionRange prune + list sub-prune
REFERENCEParent-child FK tablesInherits parent's pruning
⸻ LINUX FOR ORACLE DBAs — COMMANDS, PERMISSIONS, USERS, STORAGE & NETWORKING
▶ NAVIGATION & FILE MANAGEMENT
FILE NAVIGATION
pwdPrint working directory — full path of current location
ls -laLong listing with hidden files (permissions, owner, size, date)
ls -lhLong listing with human-readable sizes (KB/MB/GB)
cd /pathChange to absolute path
cd ~Go to home directory (same as cd $HOME)
cd -Go back to previous directory
cd ..Go up one directory level
find /u01 -name "*.dbf"Find files by name pattern — locate Oracle datafiles
find /u01 -size +500MFind files larger than 500 MB
find /path -mtime -1Files modified in last 1 day (useful after patching)
locate filenameFast search using pre-built index (updatedb refreshes it)
which sqlplusShow full path of executable — verify Oracle is in PATH
Oracle DBA standard paths: $ORACLE_BASE = /u01/app/oracle $ORACLE_HOME = /u01/app/oracle/product/19c/dbhome_1 Datafiles = /u01/app/oracle/oradata/DBNAME/ Alert log = $ORACLE_BASE/diag/rdbms/dbname/SID/trace/
FILE OPERATIONS
cp src destCopy file to destination
cp -r src/ dest/Copy directory recursively
mv src destMove or rename file/directory
rm fileDelete file permanently (no Recycle Bin!)
rm -rf dir/Recursively delete directory — DANGEROUS, no undo
mkdir -p /a/b/cCreate full directory tree (all intermediate dirs)
ln -s /target /linkCreate symbolic link (like Windows junction / shortcut)
touch fileCreate empty file or update timestamps
stat fileDetailed file metadata: size, inode, permissions, all timestamps
file binaryDetect file type: ELF binary, text, compressed, etc.
du -sh /u01Disk usage of directory in human-readable format
du -sh * | sort -rhSort subdirectories by size — find space hogs
# Find largest files in Oracle area: find /u01 -type f -size +500M -exec ls -lh {} \; # Safe delete — move to trash dir first: mv /oradata/old.dbf /tmp/trash/
▶ TEXT VIEWING & VIM EDITOR
TEXT VIEWING COMMANDS
cat filePrint entire file to screen
less filePage through file (q=quit, /=search, n=next, G=end, g=start)
head -20 fileShow first 20 lines (default 10)
tail -50 fileShow last 50 lines (default 10)
tail -f alert_orcl.logFollow file as it grows — essential for Oracle alert log monitoring
tail -100f fileLast 100 lines then follow new output
grep "ORA-" alert.logSearch for pattern — hunt Oracle errors
grep -i "error" fileCase-insensitive search
grep -r "pattern" /dirRecursive search through directory
grep -v "^#" fileExclude comment lines (show only active config)
grep -A3 -B1 "ORA-"3 lines After + 1 line Before each match (context)
wc -l fileCount lines in file
# Oracle alert log path: $ORACLE_BASE/diag/rdbms/orcl/orcl/trace/alert_orcl.log # Monitor in real-time: tail -f $ORACLE_BASE/diag/rdbms/ORCL/orcl/trace/alert_orcl.log # Count ORA- errors today: grep "ORA-" alert_orcl.log | wc -l
VIM EDITOR — ESSENTIAL COMMANDS
vim has two main modes: COMMAND (navigation/actions) and INSERT (typing text). Always opens in COMMAND mode.
ENTERING INSERT MODE
iInsert before cursor
aAppend after cursor
oOpen new line below and insert
EscReturn to COMMAND mode (always press Esc first if unsure)
COMMAND MODE — SAVE / QUIT
:wSave (write) file
:wqSave and quit
:q!Quit without saving (force quit)
NAVIGATION & EDITING
gg / GGo to top / bottom of file
:50Jump to line 50
/ORA-Search forward for pattern (n=next match, N=previous)
ddDelete (cut) current line
yy / pCopy (yank) line / Paste below cursor
u / Ctrl+RUndo / Redo
:%s/old/new/gGlobal find and replace in whole file
:set numberShow line numbers
:set nowrapDisable line wrapping (useful for wide SQL)
▶ PROCESS MANAGEMENT & SYSTEM MONITORING
PROCESS MANAGEMENT
ps auxList ALL running processes (a=all users, u=format, x=no tty)
ps -efFull-format process listing
ps -ef | grep ora_List Oracle background processes (LGWR, DBWn, CKPT, SMON, PMON...)
topReal-time process monitor — like Windows Task Manager. q=quit
top -u oracleShow only oracle user processes
htopEnhanced interactive process viewer with colors (install separately)
kill -15 PIDGraceful terminate — SIGTERM (allows cleanup, preferred)
kill -9 PIDForce-kill — SIGKILL (immediate, no cleanup — use as last resort)
killall pmonKill all processes named pmon — WARNING: kills Oracle instance
nohup cmd &Run in background, survive terminal close (HUP signal immune)
jobs / bg %1 / fg %1List jobs / resume in background / bring to foreground
Ctrl+Z / Ctrl+CSuspend process / Interrupt (terminate) process
# Verify Oracle background processes are running: ps -ef | grep -E 'ora_pmon|ora_lgwr|ora_dbw|ora_ckpt' | grep -v grep # Kill a specific Oracle shadow process (OS-level session kill): # First find the SPID from v$process, then: kill -9 <SPID>
SYSTEM MONITORING & INFO
vmstat 5 3CPU/memory/swap/IO stats every 5s, 3 iterations
iostat -x 5Per-disk I/O stats every 5s — critical for Oracle I/O tuning
sar -u 5 12CPU utilization every 5s for 1 minute (requires sysstat)
free -hRAM and swap usage in human-readable format
uptimeSystem uptime + load averages (1m / 5m / 15m)
uname -aKernel version, hostname, OS architecture
cat /proc/cpuinfoCPU model, core count, clock speed
cat /proc/meminfoDetailed RAM, swap, huge pages info
dmesg | tail -50Kernel messages — OOM killer, hardware errors, driver issues
journalctl -xesystemd journal — recent system events with explanations
systemctl status oracleOracle systemd service status (if configured)
ulimit -aShell resource limits — Oracle requires specific open files/processes settings
# Oracle kernel parameters (must be set in /etc/sysctl.conf): kernel.shmmax = 4294967296 # Shared memory max (for SGA) kernel.shmall = 2097152 # Shared memory pages kernel.shmmni = 4096 # Shared memory segments vm.swappiness = 10 # Minimize swapping (Oracle prefers RAM) fs.file-max = 6815744 # Max open files system-wide # Apply without reboot: sysctl -p # Check HugePages (critical for large SGA): grep HugePage /proc/meminfo
ARCHIVING & COMPRESSION
tar -czf arch.tar.gz /dirCreate compressed tarball (c=create, z=gzip, f=filename)
tar -xzf arch.tar.gzExtract gzip tarball (x=extract)
tar -xzf file.tar.gz -C /destExtract to specific destination directory
tar -tzf arch.tar.gzList tarball contents without extracting
gzip file / gunzip file.gzCompress / decompress a single file in-place
zcat file.gz | grep "ORA-"Read compressed file without extracting — grep archived logs
zip -r arch.zip /dirCreate zip archive recursively
unzip arch.zip -d /destExtract zip to destination
# Backup Oracle config files with timestamp: tar -czf oracle_cfg_$(date +%Y%m%d).tar.gz \ $ORACLE_HOME/dbs/spfile*.ora \ $ORACLE_HOME/network/admin/*.ora # Search archived Oracle logs without extracting: zcat /arch/1_12345_*.arc.gz 2>/dev/null | strings | grep "ORA-"
TEXT PROCESSING (awk, sed, sort, cut)
awk '{print $1,$3}' filePrint columns 1 and 3 (space-delimited)
awk -F: '{print $1}' /etc/passwdColon delimiter — print all usernames
awk 'NR>5 && NR<20' filePrint lines 6–19 (NR = line number)
awk '$5>100 {print}' fileFilter rows where column 5 is greater than 100
sed 's/old/new/g' fileReplace all occurrences (output only, file unchanged)
sed -i 's/old/new/g' fileIn-place replacement — modifies file directly
sed -n '10,20p' filePrint only lines 10–20
sed '/^#/d' fileDelete all comment lines from output
sort -k2 -n fileSort by column 2 numerically
sort -rh | head -20Sort human-size (MB/GB) descending, show top 20
cut -d: -f1,3 /etc/passwdExtract columns 1 and 3 with colon delimiter
uniq -c | sort -rnCount duplicates then sort by frequency
# Extract all ORA- errors with timestamps: grep -E "^[0-9]|ORA-" alert_orcl.log | grep -B1 "ORA-" # Top 10 wait events from v$session_wait (piped through awk): sqlplus -s / as sysdba <<EOF | awk 'NR>1 {print $0}' | sort -k3 -rn | head -10 SELECT event, count(*), avg(wait_time) FROM v\$session_wait GROUP BY event; EOF
▲ LINUX PERMISSIONS — DETAILED EXPLANATION
THE PERMISSION MODEL — HOW IT WORKS
Every Linux file and directory has an owner (user), a group, and permission bits for three principals: User/Owner (u), Group (g), and Others (o). Each principal gets 3 bits: read (r=4), write (w=2), execute (x=1).
$ ls -la /u01/app/oracle/oradata/ORCL/system01.dbf -rw-r----- 1 oracle dba 838860800 Mar 10 14:22 system01.dbf │└─┤ └─┤ └┤ │ │ │ └── Others : --- (no access at all) │ │ └───── Group : r-- (dba group can read only) │ └───────── Owner : rw- (oracle user can read + write) └──────────── File type: - = file, d = directory, l = symlink, b = block device Columns: [permissions] [hard links] [owner] [group] [size bytes] [date] [name]
BitOctalOn FilesOn Directories
r (read)4Read file contentList contents with ls
w (write)2Modify / overwrite fileCreate or delete files inside
x (execute)1Run as executablecd into directory
- (none)0Permission deniedPermission denied
OctalBinarySymbolicTypical Use
7111rwxFull access (scripts, dirs)
6110rw-Read + Write (datafiles)
5101r-xRead + Execute (shared bin)
4100r--Read only (config files)
0000---No access (others on DBFs)
chmod — CHANGE PERMISSIONS
OCTAL MODE — sets exact permission bits
chmod 755 script.shrwxr-xr-x — owner all, group/others read+execute
chmod 644 file.txtrw-r--r-- — owner rw, others read only
chmod 640 datafile.dbfrw-r----- — Oracle standard for datafiles
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_rsarw------- — SSH private key (SSH rejects looser permissions)
chmod 750 $ORACLE_HOMErwxr-x--- — ORACLE_HOME standard
chmod -R 750 /u01Recursively apply to entire directory tree
SYMBOLIC MODE — adds or removes bits
chmod u+x script.shAdd execute for owner (u=user/owner)
chmod g-w fileRemove write from group
chmod o-rx fileRemove read+execute from others
chmod a+r fileAdd read for all (a = u+g+o)
Oracle standard permissions: $ORACLE_HOME 750 oracle:oinstall (rwxr-x---) datafiles .dbf 640 oracle:dba (rw-r-----) redo logs .log 640 oracle:dba (rw-r-----) control files 640 oracle:dba (rw-r-----) oracle binary 6751 root:oinstall (rwsr-xr-x) <-- SUID set
chown, chgrp, umask & SPECIAL BITS
OWNERSHIP
chown oracle fileChange owner to oracle user
chown oracle:dba fileChange owner AND group in one command
chown -R oracle:oinstall /u01Recursively change ownership of entire tree
