chgrp dba /oradataChange group only
SPECIAL PERMISSION BITS
SUID bit (4___)Execute runs as FILE OWNER regardless of caller. Set on oracle binary (rwsr-xr-x) so any OS user can start it with oracle privileges. chmod 4755 file
SGID bit (_2__)New files in directory inherit directory GROUP. Essential for /oradata — all files get group dba automatically. chmod 2755 dir (shows rwxr-sr-x)
Sticky bit (__1_)In shared directory, only file OWNER can delete their files. Used on /tmp. chmod 1777 dir (shows rwxrwxrwt)
umask — DEFAULT NEW FILE PERMISSIONS
umask subtracts from maximum: 666 for files, 777 for dirs. Oracle recommends umask 022.
umaskShow current umask (022 = subtract rw from group/others)
umask 022Set umask — add to .bash_profile for persistence
umask 022 math: New file: 666 - 022 = 644 → rw-r--r-- New dir: 777 - 022 = 755 → rwxr-xr-x # ACL — fine-grained permissions beyond owner/group/others: getfacl /oradata # View ACL entries setfacl -m u:grid:rwx /asm # Grant grid user rwx setfacl -m g:dba:r-- /logs # Grant dba group read-only setfacl -x u:grid /asm # Remove grid ACL entry
◆ LINUX USER MANAGEMENT — DETAILED EXPLANATION
USER & GROUP FILES — ANATOMY
Linux stores accounts in plain text files. Understanding the structure is fundamental — especially for setting up Oracle OS users correctly.
/etc/passwd — USER ACCOUNTS (world-readable)
oracle:x:54321:54321:Oracle DB:/home/oracle:/bin/bash │ │ │ │ │ │ └─ login shell │ │ │ │ │ └─────────── home directory │ │ │ │ └───────────────────── GECOS comment field │ │ │ └─────────────────────────── primary GID │ │ └───────────────────────────────── UID │ └─────────────────────────────────── 'x' = password in shadow └────────────────────────────────────────── username
/etc/shadow — HASHED PASSWORDS (root-only)
oracle:$6$salt$hash:19000:0:99999:7::: │ │ │ │ │ └─ warn days before expiry │ │ │ │ └─────── max password age (days) │ │ │ └───────── min days between changes │ │ └─────────────── last change (days since epoch) │ └──────────────────────────────────── SHA-512 password hash └─────────────────────────────────────────── username
/etc/group — GROUPS
dba:x:54322:oracle,grid │ │ │ └─ supplementary members (comma-separated) │ │ └─────── GID │ └─────────── no group password └─────────────── group name
USER MANAGEMENT COMMANDS
CREATE
useradd oracleCreate user (home auto-created, default shell /bin/sh)
useradd -u 54321 -g oinstall -G dba,oper,backupdba -d /home/oracle -s /bin/bash oracleFull Oracle user creation: specific UID, primary group, supplementary groups, home, shell
passwd oracleSet password for oracle user (root can set any user)
MODIFY
usermod -aG dba oracleAppend oracle to dba group. The -a flag is CRITICAL — without it, replaces ALL groups
usermod -s /bin/bash oracleChange login shell
usermod -L oracleLock account (disables password login)
usermod -U oracleUnlock account
usermod -e 2027-12-31 oracleSet account expiry date
QUERY & DELETE
id oracleShow UID, GID, all supplementary groups
groups oracleList all groups oracle belongs to
whoami / who / wCurrent user / all logged-in users / users + what they run
last -20Last 20 login events from /var/log/wtmp
userdel -r oracleDelete user AND home directory (-r flag)
sudo — PRIVILEGE ESCALATION
sudo lets authorized users run commands as root. Configured in /etc/sudoers — ALWAYS edit with visudo (syntax-checks before saving). Equivalent to Windows UAC + "Run as Administrator".
sudo commandRun command as root (prompts for YOUR password)
sudo -u oracle sqlplus /Run as oracle user (identity switch without su)
sudo -iOpen interactive root shell with root environment
sudo -lList all sudo commands current user is allowed to run
su - oracleSwitch to oracle user WITH login shell — loads .bash_profile, sets ORACLE_SID
su oracleSwitch user WITHOUT login shell — .bash_profile NOT loaded. ORACLE_SID may be unset!
# /etc/sudoers (ONLY edit with visudo): # Grant oracle full root access (not recommended in prod): oracle ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: ALL # Restrict to specific commands only (preferred): oracle ALL=(root) NOPASSWD: /sbin/mount, /sbin/umount, /etc/init.d/oracle # Allow without TTY (for automated scripts): Defaults:oracle !requiretty # Edit: visudo # validates syntax before saving — never edit directly!
ORACLE OS GROUPS — REQUIRED SETUP
Oracle requires specific OS groups that map to internal database roles. These must exist BEFORE the Oracle installer runs.
OS GroupMaps to Oracle RolePurpose
oinstallOracle InventoryPrimary group for all Oracle users (oracle, grid). Required for installer
dbaSYSDBAFull DBA access — CONNECT AS SYSDBA (≈ sysadmin in SQL Server)
operSYSOPERStartup/shutdown only — no access to user data
backupdbaSYSBACKUPRMAN backup and restore operations
dgdbaSYSDGData Guard administration via DGMGRL
kmdbaSYSKMTDE encryption key management
asmadminSYSASMASM instance admin (grid user only)
asmdbaASM SYSDBAoracle user needs this to access ASM disk groups
# Create all Oracle groups: groupadd -g 54321 oinstall ; groupadd -g 54322 dba groupadd -g 54323 oper ; groupadd -g 54324 backupdba groupadd -g 54325 dgdba ; groupadd -g 54326 kmdba groupadd -g 54327 asmadmin ; groupadd -g 54328 asmdba # Create oracle user with all required groups: useradd -u 54321 -g oinstall \ -G dba,oper,backupdba,dgdba,kmdba,asmdba \ -d /home/oracle -s /bin/bash oracle
■ LINUX STORAGE MANAGEMENT — DETAILED EXPLANATION
DISK & FILESYSTEM INSPECTION
df -hDisk space of all mounted filesystems (human-readable)
df -hTInclude filesystem type (xfs, ext4, nfs, tmpfs)
df -hiInode usage — can run out of inodes while disk space remains
lsblkBlock device tree — disks, partitions, LVM layers
lsblk -fInclude filesystem type, UUID, and mount point
blkidShow UUID and FS type for all block devices
du -sh /u01/*Disk usage per subdirectory under /u01
fdisk -lList all disks and partition tables (requires root)
parted -lList partitions — supports GPT + MBR, handles disks >2TB
pvs / vgs / lvsLVM summary: Physical Volumes / Volume Groups / Logical Volumes
# Pre-flight check before adding Oracle datafile: df -h /u01/app/oracle/oradata/ # Find largest files consuming Oracle space: find /u01 -type f -size +500M -exec ls -lh {} \; | sort -k5 -rh # Check available LVM space before extending: vgs vg_oracle
MOUNT / UMOUNT & /etc/fstab
mountShow all currently mounted filesystems
mount /dev/sdb1 /mnt/dataMount device to mount point
mount -t xfs /dev/sdb1 /dataMount specifying filesystem type explicitly
umount /mnt/dataUnmount filesystem (device must not be in use)
fuser -m /mnt/dataShow processes using mount point (before umount)
mount -aMount all entries in /etc/fstab (test fstab without reboot)
# /etc/fstab — persistent mount configuration: # Device MountPt FSType Options Dump Pass /dev/sda1 / xfs defaults 0 1 UUID=abc123 /u01 xfs defaults,noatime 0 2 /dev/sdb /oradata xfs defaults,noatime 0 2 /swapfile none swap sw 0 0 # Key mount options for Oracle: # noatime = no access-time update (better I/O for DB workloads) # noexec = prevent binaries executing (security for data dirs) # nofail = don't halt boot if device missing # NFS mount for RAC shared storage: nfssvr:/export/oradata /u02/oradata nfs \ rw,bg,hard,nointr,rsize=32768,wsize=32768,timeo=600 0 0
LVM — LOGICAL VOLUME MANAGER
LVM adds a virtualization layer: PV (Physical Volume — raw disk) → VG (Volume Group — pool of PVs) → LV (Logical Volume — mountable slice). Equivalent to Windows Storage Spaces / Dynamic Disks.
PHYSICAL VOLUMES (PV)
pvcreate /dev/sdbInitialize disk as LVM Physical Volume
pvs / pvdisplayList PVs / show detailed PV info including free PE
VOLUME GROUPS (VG)
vgcreate vg_oracle /dev/sdbCreate Volume Group from PV
vgextend vg_oracle /dev/sdcAdd another disk to expand VG
vgs / vgdisplayVG summary / detailed info with free space
LOGICAL VOLUMES (LV)
lvcreate -L 200G -n lv_data vg_oracleCreate 200 GB LV named lv_data
lvcreate -l 100%FREE -n lv_redo vg_oracleUse all remaining free space
lvextend -L +100G /dev/vg_oracle/lv_dataExtend LV by 100 GB (then grow filesystem)
lvextend -l +100%FREE /dev/vg_oracle/lv_dataExtend using all remaining free space in VG
lvs / lvdisplayLV summary / full details
# Full workflow: add 200 GB to Oracle data area: pvcreate /dev/sdc vgextend vg_oracle /dev/sdc lvextend -L +200G /dev/vg_oracle/lv_oradata # Grow the filesystem online (no downtime): xfs_growfs /u01/app/oracle/oradata # XFS (cannot shrink) # OR for ext4: resize2fs /dev/vg_oracle/lv_oradata # ext4 (can grow or shrink)
FILESYSTEM CREATION, SWAP & XFS vs EXT4
CREATE FILESYSTEMS (mkfs)
mkfs.xfs /dev/vg_oracle/lv_dataCreate XFS (preferred for Oracle on RHEL 7+)
mkfs.ext4 /dev/sdb1Create ext4 (traditional Linux default)
mkfs.xfs -f /dev/sdbForce format — overwrite existing filesystem
XFS vs EXT4 FOR ORACLE
FeatureXFSEXT4
Oracle recommendationPreferred (RHEL 7+)Supported
Max file size8 EB16 TB
Online growYes (xfs_growfs)Yes (resize2fs)
Online shrinkNot supportedYes
Large file I/OExcellentGood
SWAP MANAGEMENT
swapon -s / free -hShow swap devices / RAM and swap usage
swapoff /dev/sda2Disable swap partition
swapon /dev/sda2Enable swap partition
# Oracle swap requirement: ≥ 1.5x RAM (up to 16 GB) # Create swap file if partition not available: dd if=/dev/zero of=/swapfile bs=1G count=16 chmod 600 /swapfile mkswap /swapfile swapon /swapfile echo "/swapfile none swap sw 0 0" >> /etc/fstab
■ LINUX NETWORKING — DETAILED EXPLANATION
IP & INTERFACE MANAGEMENT
Modern Linux uses iproute2 (ip command). Legacy ifconfig/route (net-tools) is deprecated but still common on older systems.
MODERN — ip command (iproute2)
ip addr showShow all interfaces with IP addresses (short: ip a)
ip addr show eth0Show single interface details
ip link showLink layer: MAC, MTU, state UP/DOWN
ip link set eth0 up/downEnable or disable interface
ip route showRouting table (short: ip r)
ip route add default via 192.168.1.1Add default gateway (session only)
ip addr add 10.0.0.10/24 dev eth0Add IP address — temporary (lost on reboot)
LEGACY — ifconfig (net-tools)
ifconfigShow active interfaces (install net-tools if missing)
ifconfig eth0 up/downEnable/disable interface
# Persistent config — RHEL 7 (/etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ifcfg-eth0): TYPE=Ethernet ; BOOTPROTO=none IPADDR=192.168.1.10 ; PREFIX=24 ; GATEWAY=192.168.1.1 DNS1=192.168.1.53 ; ONBOOT=yes # RHEL 8+ uses NetworkManager: nmcli device show eth0 nmcli connection modify eth0 ipv4.addresses 192.168.1.10/24 nmcli connection up eth0 # Oracle RAC network requirements: # 1. Public interface — client / application traffic # 2. Private interface — interconnect (Cache Fusion, no routing) # 3. Virtual IPs (VIP) — managed by Grid Infrastructure / Clusterware # 4. SCAN IPs (3 recommended) — single client access name
CONNECTIVITY TESTING
ping -c 4 hostnameTest ICMP reachability — 4 packets (Ctrl+C to stop)
traceroute hostShow network path hop-by-hop to destination
tracepath hostTraceroute alternative (no root required)
mtr hostReal-time ping + traceroute combined — great for latency analysis
telnet dbhost 1521Test TCP port 1521 — Oracle listener connectivity check
nc -zv host 1521Netcat port scan (z=connect only, v=verbose) — faster than telnet
nslookup hostnameDNS forward/reverse lookup (basic)
dig hostnameDetailed DNS query with TTL, record type, server used
dig -x 192.168.1.10Reverse DNS lookup (IP → hostname)
host hostnameSimple DNS lookup — A, MX, PTR records
# Oracle listener port tests: telnet db-server 1521 # Opens: Escape character is '^]' # Oracle tnsping — tests full TNS chain: tnsping ORCL # Resolves service, pings listener # Full TNS connect string test (bypass tnsnames.ora): sqlplus user/pass@"(DESCRIPTION=(ADDRESS=(PROTOCOL=TCP)\ (HOST=dbhost)(PORT=1521))(CONNECT_DATA=(SERVICE_NAME=ORCL)))" # Test EM Express HTTPS port: curl -sk https://dbhost:5500/em/ | head -5
SOCKET & PORT MONITORING (ss / netstat)
ss is the modern replacement for netstat. Both show connections, listening ports, and socket states.
ss (modern — iproute2)
ss -tlnpTCP listening ports with PID/process (t=tcp, l=listen, n=numeric, p=process)
ss -tulnpTCP + UDP listening ports with process info
ss -an | grep 1521All socket states on Oracle listener port
ss -tpEstablished TCP connections with process names
ss -sSummary statistics of socket counts per state
netstat (legacy — net-tools)
netstat -tlnpTCP listening ports with PID (same as ss -tlnp)
netstat -an | grep 1521All connections on port 1521
netstat -rnRouting table in numeric format
# Verify Oracle listener is up on port 1521: ss -tlnp | grep 1521 # Output: LISTEN 0 128 0.0.0.0:1521 0.0.0.0:* users:(("tnslsnr",pid=1234,fd=7)) # Count active Oracle connections: ss -an | grep ":1521" | grep -c ESTAB # Real-time connection watch: watch -n 2 "ss -an | grep ':1521' | grep -c ESTAB" # Find all ports Oracle is listening on: ss -tlnp | grep -E "tnslsnr|oracle"
SSH, SCP, RSYNC & FILE TRANSFER
SSH — SECURE SHELL
ssh oracle@dbhostConnect to remote host as oracle user
ssh -p 2222 oracle@hostNon-standard SSH port
ssh -i ~/.ssh/id_rsa oracle@hostSpecific private key — passwordless auth
ssh -L 1521:dbhost:1521 user@jumpTunnel: forward local port 1521 through jump host to DB
ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096Generate SSH key pair (required for RAC passwordless SSH)
ssh-copy-id oracle@node2Copy public key to remote authorized_keys (RAC setup)
SCP & RSYNC
scp file.dmp oracle@host:/u01/dpump/Copy file to remote host securely
scp oracle@host:/u01/dump.dmp .Download file from remote host
scp -r /dir oracle@host:/dest/Recursive directory copy
rsync -avz /src/ oracle@host:/dest/Sync — only transfers changed files (ideal for large Data Pump dumps)
rsync -avz --progress /u01/ node2:/u01/Sync with progress display
# RAC passwordless SSH setup (required for Grid installer): # On each node, as oracle and grid users: ssh-keygen -t rsa # Accept defaults (no passphrase) ssh-copy-id oracle@node1 # Copy to self ssh-copy-id oracle@node2 # Copy to other node # Verify: ssh node2 "hostname; id" # Must work without password prompt # Transfer Data Pump export to target: rsync -avz --progress /u01/dpump/export.dmp oracle@target:/u01/dpump/
FIREWALL MANAGEMENT
RHEL 7+ uses firewalld (managed via firewall-cmd). RHEL 6 used iptables directly. Oracle ports that must be open: 1521 (listener), 5500 (EM Express), 7803 (GI HTTP), 2483 (TCPS), 6200 (SCAN).
firewalld (RHEL 7+)
firewall-cmd --stateCheck if firewalld is running
firewall-cmd --list-allList active zone, services, and open ports
firewall-cmd --add-port=1521/tcp --permanentOpen Oracle listener port permanently
firewall-cmd --add-port=5500/tcp --permanentOpen EM Express port permanently
firewall-cmd --reloadApply --permanent changes (required after every permanent change)
firewall-cmd --remove-port=1521/tcp --permanentClose a port permanently
iptables (RHEL 6 / legacy)
iptables -L -n -vList all rules with packet/byte counters
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 1521 -j ACCEPTAllow inbound Oracle connections
service iptables savePersist rules across reboots
# Open all Oracle-required ports at once: for PORT in 1521 5500 7803 2483 6200; do firewall-cmd --permanent --add-port=${PORT}/tcp done firewall-cmd --reload # Disable firewall for dev/test only: systemctl stop firewalld && systemctl disable firewalld
KEY NETWORK CONFIG FILES
/etc/hosts — STATIC DNS (checked before DNS server)
# CRITICAL for Oracle RAC — all nodes, VIPs, SCAN must be here: 192.168.1.10 rac1 rac1.example.com 192.168.1.11 rac2 rac2.example.com 192.168.1.12 rac1-vip rac1-vip.example.com 192.168.1.13 rac2-vip rac2-vip.example.com 192.168.1.20 rac-scan rac-scan.example.com 10.10.0.10 rac1-priv # Private interconnect 10.10.0.11 rac2-priv # Private interconnect
/etc/resolv.conf — DNS SERVERS
search example.com # Default search domain nameserver 192.168.1.53 # Primary DNS nameserver 8.8.8.8 # Secondary (fallback)
/etc/nsswitch.conf — NAME RESOLUTION ORDER
hosts: files dns # Check /etc/hosts FIRST, then DNS # Oracle RAC requires "files dns" — NEVER "dns files"
curl & wget — HTTP TOOLS
curl -O https://url/patch.zipDownload file (like wget but more powerful)
curl -sk https://host:5500/em/Test EM Express HTTPS (-s=silent, -k=skip cert check)
wget https://url/file.zipDownload with progress bar (resumes with -c)
wget -q --spider https://urlTest URL accessibility without downloading
◆ ORACLE ENVIRONMENT VARIABLES & .bash_profile — THE #1 GOTCHA FOR SQL SERVER DBAs
REQUIRED ORACLE ENVIRONMENT (set in /home/oracle/.bash_profile)
SQL Server DBAs beware: In SQL Server, you connect through SSMS/connection strings — the OS environment doesn't matter. In Oracle, you MUST set these shell variables correctly BEFORE running sqlplus, rman, expdp, or any Oracle tool. Wrong ORACLE_SID = wrong instance. Missing ORACLE_HOME = "command not found".
# ── /home/oracle/.bash_profile ───────────────────────────────────────────── # Set once here; loaded automatically at every SSH login and su - oracle export ORACLE_BASE=/u01/app/oracle export ORACLE_HOME=$ORACLE_BASE/product/19c/dbhome_1 export ORACLE_SID=ORCL # Instance name — must match init.ora / spfile export PATH=$ORACLE_HOME/bin:$PATH # Oracle binaries (sqlplus, rman, expdp...) export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=$ORACLE_HOME/lib:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH export NLS_DATE_FORMAT="YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS" # Date display in sqlplus export NLS_LANG=AMERICAN_AMERICA.AL32UTF8 # Character set export TNS_ADMIN=$ORACLE_HOME/network/admin # tnsnames.ora / listener.ora location export ORACLE_UNQNAME=ORCL # Used by Enterprise Manager # ── Verify environment is correct ────────────────────────────────────────── echo $ORACLE_SID # Must show instance name (e.g., ORCL) echo $ORACLE_HOME # Must be valid path sqlplus / as sysdba # If env correct, connects immediately with OS auth # ── Switching between instances on same host ─────────────────────────────── export ORACLE_SID=PROD # Switch to PROD instance export ORACLE_SID=DEV # Switch to DEV instance export ORACLE_SID=CDB1 # Switch to CDB (Multitenant) # ── su - vs su (critical difference) ───────────────────────────────────── su - oracle # Login shell: .bash_profile loaded → ORACLE_SID is set ✓ su oracle # Non-login: .bash_profile NOT loaded → ORACLE_SID unset ✗ # ── Common mistake fix ──────────────────────────────────────────────────── # Error: "ORA-12162: TNS:net service name is incorrectly specified" # Cause: ORACLE_SID not set # Fix: export ORACLE_SID=ORCL (then retry)
◆ LINUX QUICK REFERENCE — SQL SERVER DBA MAPPING
WINDOWS / SQL SERVER ADMIN → LINUX EQUIVALENT
Windows / SQL Server ConceptLinux EquivalentNotes
File Explorerls, cd, findNo GUI by default — command line navigation
Task Managertop / htop / ps auxtop is built-in; htop requires install
Event Viewerjournalctl / /var/log/messagesjournalctl -xe for recent errors
Services (services.msc)systemctl start/stop/statussystemctl is systemd service manager
Run as Administratorsudo commandConfigured per-user in /etc/sudoers
Add/Remove Programsyum / dnf / rpmyum install package / yum remove package
Disk Managementlsblk / fdisk / LVM toolsLVM = Storage Spaces / Dynamic Disks
Recycle BinNone (rm is permanent)Use mv to /tmp/trash/ as safety habit
Notepad / VSCodevim / nano / geditvim is universal; nano is simpler
cmd / PowerShellbash shellMore powerful scripting via bash
ipconfigip addr / ifconfigip addr is modern equivalent
netstat -anss -tulnp / netstat -tulnpss is faster and more detailed
ping / tracertping / tracerouteSame concept, traceroute = tracert
Windows Firewallfirewall-cmd / iptablesfirewalld on RHEL 7+
NTFS permissionschmod / chown / ACLUnix: rwx per owner/group/others + ACL
Environment Variablesexport VAR=valueIn .bash_profile for persistence
Task Schedulercron / crontabcrontab -e to edit; crontab -l to list
xcopy / robocopycp -r / rsyncrsync is far more powerful than robocopy
COURSE INTRO SQL SERVER TO ORACLE DBA — 5-DAY TRANSITION COURSE
🎓 COURSE OVERVIEW
Duration
5 Days / 40 Hours (instructor-led with labs)
Target Audience
Experienced SQL Server DBAs (2+ years)
Prerequisite
Solid SQL Server DBA knowledge
Oracle Version
Oracle Database 12c/19c/21c
Modules
30 modules across 5 days
Philosophy
Every Oracle concept taught by comparison to SQL Server equivalent
🎯 CERTIFICATION PATH
1Z0-082
Oracle DB Admin I — Core DBA: architecture, storage, security, backup
1Z0-083
Oracle DB Admin II — Advanced: HA, performance, RMAN, partitioning
1Z0-071
Oracle DB SQL — SQL proficiency, PL/SQL basics
Recommended order: 1Z0-082 → 1Z0-071 → 1Z0-083
💻 PRACTICE ENVIRONMENT
Oracle 19c/21c
Free download from oracle.com/database
Oracle LiveSQL
livesql.oracle.com — free browser-based Oracle SQL
VirtualBox VM
Oracle pre-built appliance — quickest lab setup
SQL Developer
Free GUI tool (equivalent to SSMS)
SQL*Plus
Command-line tool (equivalent to sqlcmd)
30 MODULES 5-DAY CURRICULUM OVERVIEW
DayModuleTopicDuration
Day 1M1Architecture Deep Dive — SGA, PGA, Background Processes2h
Day 1M2Multitenant Architecture — CDB, PDB, CDB$ROOT1h
Day 1M3Installation & Configuration — DBCA, env vars, directory structure1h
Day 1M4Initialization Parameters — PFILE, SPFILE, ALTER SYSTEM SET1.5h
Day 1M5Startup & Shutdown — 4 stages, shutdown modes1.5h
Day 1M6Network Configuration — listener.ora, tnsnames.ora, sqlnet.ora1h
Day 2M7Storage Architecture — Tablespaces, datafiles, segments, ASM2h
Day 2M8Redo Logs & Archive Logs — circular logging, ARCHIVELOG mode1h
Day 2M9Control Files & Alert Log — multiplexing, V$DIAG_INFO1h
Day 2M10Data Type Mapping — NUMBER, VARCHAR2, DATE gotcha1.5h
Day 2M11Core SQL Syntax Differences — SYSDATE, NVL, ||, DUAL, FETCH FIRST1.5h
Day 2M12DDL & DML Differences — CREATE TABLE, sequences, INSERT ALL, MERGE1h
Day 3M13Transaction Model — no auto-commit, DDL commits, SAVEPOINT1.5h
Day 3M14Concurrency & Locking — MVCC, row-level locks, ORA-015551.5h
Day 3M15PL/SQL Fundamentals — block structure, variables, control flow1h
Day 3M16PL/SQL Procedures, Functions & Packages2h
Day 3M17Error Handling & Triggers — EXCEPTION WHEN, :OLD/:NEW1h
Day 3M18Security & User Management — User=Schema, privs, roles, auditing1h
Day 4M19RMAN Fundamentals — connect, full/incremental, validate, retention2h
Day 4M20Recovery Scenarios — complete, PITR, tablespace, datafile1.5h
Day 4M21Flashback Technologies — Query, Table, Drop, Database0.5h
Day 4M22Data Pump & SQL*Loader — expdp/impdp, REMAP_SCHEMA1h
Day 4M23Performance — EXPLAIN PLAN, DBMS_XPLAN, hints, DBMS_STATS1.5h
Day 4M24AWR, ASH & ADDM — snapshots, reports, wait events1.5h
Day 5M25Indexes & Partitioning — B-tree, bitmap, IOT, range/list/hash1.5h
Day 5M26Data Guard — physical standby, protection modes, switchover1.5h
Day 5M27Oracle RAC — active-active, Cache Fusion, SCAN listener1h
Day 5M28Daily Operations Toolkit — SQL*Plus, EM, DBMS_SCHEDULER1.5h
Day 5M29Advanced Features — DB Links, Materialized Views, Synonyms1h
Day 5M30Comprehensive Review & Assessment — 50 MCQ + practical exam1.5h
QUICK MAP SQL SERVER ↔ ORACLE CONCEPT MAPPING
ARCHITECTURE & MEMORY
SQL ServerOracle Equivalent
Buffer PoolDatabase Buffer Cache (SGA)
Plan CacheShared Pool / Library Cache
Single process, multi-threadMulti-process (PMON, SMON, DBWn…)
TempDBTemporary Tablespace
Contained DatabasePDB (Pluggable Database)
STORAGE & LOGGING
SQL ServerOracle Equivalent
Filegroup (.mdf/.ndf)Tablespace (datafiles)
Transaction Log (.ldf)Redo Log + Archive Log
FULL Recovery ModelARCHIVELOG Mode
CheckpointCheckpoint (CKPT process)
sys.master_filesV$DATAFILE / DBA_DATA_FILES
TOOLS & MANAGEMENT
SQL ServerOracle Equivalent
SSMSSQL Developer / Enterprise Manager
sqlcmdSQL*Plus / SQLcl
SQL Server AgentDBMS_SCHEDULER
sp_configureALTER SYSTEM SET / SPFILE
sys.dm_* (DMVs)V$* views + DBA_* views
HA, BACKUP & SECURITY
SQL ServerOracle Equivalent
Always On AGData Guard
Failover Cluster InstanceRAC (Real Application Clusters)
BACKUP DATABASE (T-SQL)RMAN BACKUP DATABASE
BCP / SSISData Pump (expdp/impdp)
Schema (owned by login)User = Schema (same object)
DAY 1 · M1 INSTANCE vs DATABASE — THE CRITICAL DISTINCTION
SQL SERVER MODEL
Single process, multiple threads — everything in sqlservr.exe
Process model
One OS process (sqlservr.exe) with worker threads
Memory
Single contiguous buffer pool shared by all databases
1 instance
Always serves exactly 1 set of databases
Log writer
Thread inside sqlservr.exe
ORACLE MODEL
Multiple OS processes — each background process is a separate OS process
Process model
Many OS processes: DBWn, LGWR, PMON, SMON, CKPT…
Memory
SGA (shared) + PGA (per session, private)
Instance : Database
1 instance ↔ 1 database (non-RAC) or N instances ↔ 1 DB (RAC)
LGWR
Dedicated OS process for log writing
DAY 1 · M1 SGA — SYSTEM GLOBAL AREA (SHARED BY ALL SESSIONS)
SGA COMPONENTS
Database Buffer Cache
Caches data blocks from disk — equivalent to SQL Server Buffer Pool
Shared Pool
Library Cache (parsed SQL / execution plans = Plan Cache) + Dictionary Cache (metadata)
Redo Log Buffer
Circular buffer holding redo records before LGWR flushes to disk
Large Pool
Used by RMAN, shared server, parallel query — offloads from Shared Pool
Java Pool
JVM memory for Java stored procedures
Streams Pool
Used by Oracle Streams / Golden Gate replication
AUTO MANAGEMENT
-- Let Oracle manage SGA+PGA sizing automatically: ALTER SYSTEM SET MEMORY_TARGET = 4G SCOPE=SPFILE; -- Or manage SGA only: ALTER SYSTEM SET SGA_TARGET = 3G SCOPE=BOTH; ALTER SYSTEM SET PGA_AGGREGATE_TARGET = 1G SCOPE=BOTH;
PGA — PROGRAM GLOBAL AREA (PRIVATE PER SESSION)
Not part of SGA — private to each server process / session
Sort Area
Memory for sort operations (ORDER BY, GROUP BY)
Hash Area
Memory for hash join operations
Session Info
Bind variables, cursor state, session-level data
SQL Server equiv
Worker thread stack + query workspace memory (per-query grant)
-- View current memory allocation SELECT component, current_size/1024/1024 mb FROM v$sga_dynamic_components; SELECT name, value/1024/1024 mb FROM v$pgastat WHERE name IN ('total PGA allocated','total PGA used for auto workareas');
DAY 1 · M1 KEY BACKGROUND PROCESSES
ProcessFull NameRoleSQL Server Equivalent
PMONProcess MonitorCleans up failed user processes; releases locks & resourcesDead connection cleanup (lazy writer + service broker cleanup)
SMONSystem MonitorInstance recovery on startup; coalesces free space in tablespacesSQL Server recovery process at startup
DBWnDatabase Writer (n=0-9,a-j)Writes dirty buffers from Buffer Cache to datafilesLazy Writer + Checkpoint Writer threads
LGWRLog WriterWrites redo from Redo Log Buffer to online redo log filesSQL Server Log Writer thread (flushes LDF)
CKPTCheckpointSignals DBWn to write; updates datafile headers & control file with SCNSQL Server Checkpoint thread
ARCnArchiver (n=0-4)Copies filled online redo logs to archive destination (enables PITR)SQL Server log backup process (logically)
MMONManageability MonitorTakes AWR snapshots every 60 minutes; writes metrics to SYSAUXSQL Server Query Store background capture
MMANMemory ManagerDynamically resizes SGA components under MEMORY_TARGET/SGA_TARGETSQL Server dynamic memory manager
RECORecovererResolves in-doubt distributed transactions (2PC failures)MSDTC recovery thread
CJQ0Job Queue CoordinatorSpawns Jnnn processes for DBMS_SCHEDULER jobsSQL Server Agent main thread
DAY 1 · M2 MULTITENANT ARCHITECTURE — CDB AND PDB
CDB COMPONENTS
CDB$ROOT
The root container — holds data dictionary, common users (C## prefix). Equivalent to SQL Server master/msdb combined
PDB$SEED
Read-only template for new PDBs — equivalent to SQL Server model database
PDB1, PDB2…
User pluggable databases — each equivalent to a SQL Server user database
Shared SGA
One SGA / one set of background processes serves ALL PDBs
CON_ID
Container ID: ROOT=1, SEED=2, PDB1=3, PDB2=4…
KEY PDB COMMANDS
-- Connect to CDB root as sysdba sqlplus / as sysdba -- List all containers SELECT name, con_id, open_mode FROM v$pdbs; -- Switch to a PDB ALTER SESSION SET CONTAINER = pdb1; -- Open a PDB ALTER PLUGGABLE DATABASE pdb1 OPEN; -- Save state (auto-open on restart) ALTER PLUGGABLE DATABASE pdb1 SAVE STATE; -- Create a new PDB from seed CREATE PLUGGABLE DATABASE pdb3 ADMIN USER pdb3admin IDENTIFIED BY SecurePass1 FILE_NAME_CONVERT = ('/pdbseed/', '/pdb3/'); -- Drop a PDB DROP PLUGGABLE DATABASE pdb3 INCLUDING DATAFILES;
DAY 1 · M3 INSTALLATION & ENVIRONMENT
ORACLE ENVIRONMENT VARIABLES
ORACLE_HOME
Oracle software directory — /u01/app/oracle/product/19c/dbhome_1
ORACLE_SID
Database instance identifier — equivalent to SQL Server instance name
ORACLE_BASE
Base directory for all Oracle software — /u01/app/oracle
TNS_ADMIN
Location of tnsnames.ora, listener.ora, sqlnet.ora
## ~/.bash_profile for oracle OS user export ORACLE_BASE=/u01/app/oracle export ORACLE_HOME=$ORACLE_BASE/product/19c/dbhome_1 export ORACLE_SID=ORCL export PATH=$ORACLE_HOME/bin:$PATH export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=$ORACLE_HOME/lib
KEY ORACLE DIRECTORIES
$ORACLE_HOME/bin
Executables: sqlplus, rman, expdp, impdp, lsnrctl
$ORACLE_HOME/network/admin
listener.ora, tnsnames.ora, sqlnet.ora
$ORACLE_BASE/diag/rdbms
Diagnostic repository — alert log, trace files
$ORACLE_BASE/admin/SID
adump (audit), bdump (background), cdump (core)
-- Find alert log location from SQL*Plus: SELECT value FROM v$diag_info WHERE name = 'Diag Trace'; -- DBCA: Database Configuration Assistant dbca -silent -createDatabase \ -templateName General_Purpose.dbc \ -gdbName ORCL -sid ORCL \ -sysPassword Oracle1 -systemPassword Oracle1
DAY 1 · M4 INIT PARAMETERS — PFILE vs SPFILE
PFILE vs SPFILE
PFILE (init.ora)
Plain text — manually edited, read at startup, not updated by ALTER SYSTEM
SPFILE
Binary server-side — updated by ALTER SYSTEM, preferred in production
SQL Server equiv
PFILE ≈ startup flags; SPFILE ≈ sp_configure (persisted in master DB)
-- Create SPFILE from PFILE CREATE SPFILE FROM PFILE='/u01/init.ora'; -- Create PFILE from SPFILE (for editing) CREATE PFILE='/tmp/init_backup.ora' FROM SPFILE; -- Show parameter location SHOW PARAMETER spfile; SELECT name, value, description FROM v$parameter WHERE name LIKE '%memory%';
KEY INITIALIZATION PARAMETERS
ParameterDescriptionSQL Server Equiv
MEMORY_TARGETTotal DB memory (SGA+PGA auto-managed)max server memory
SGA_TARGETAuto-tune SGA componentsmax server memory
PGA_AGGREGATE_TARGETTotal PGA limit across all sessionsmin memory per query
PROCESSESMax OS processes (sessions ≈ PROCESSES*0.9)max connections
DB_BLOCK_SIZEBlock size in bytes (default 8K), set at creationpage size (always 8K)
OPEN_CURSORSMax open cursors per sessionN/A
UNDO_TABLESPACEName of active UNDO tablespacetempdb (undo in version store)
LOG_ARCHIVE_DEST_1Archive log destinationlog backup path
-- Modify parameter (memory only — no restart) ALTER SYSTEM SET OPEN_CURSORS=500 SCOPE=MEMORY; -- Persist to SPFILE only (takes effect after restart) ALTER SYSTEM SET PROCESSES=500 SCOPE=SPFILE; -- Both (applies now AND persists) ALTER SYSTEM SET SGA_TARGET=3G SCOPE=BOTH;
DAY 1 · M5 STARTUP & SHUTDOWN STAGES
THE 4 STARTUP STAGES
StageWhat HappensUsed For
SHUTDOWNNo instance, no database mountedStarting point
NOMOUNTReads SPFILE/PFILE, creates SGA, starts background processesRecreating control file, CREATE DATABASE
MOUNTReads control file — knows datafile/redo log locationsARCHIVELOG enable, RMAN full recovery, RESETLOGS
OPENOpens & verifies all datafiles and redo logs — online to usersNormal operation
STARTUP NOMOUNT; -- read SPFILE, start SGA+BG procs ALTER DATABASE MOUNT; -- read control file ALTER DATABASE OPEN; -- open all files STARTUP; -- shortcut: NOMOUNT→MOUNT→OPEN STARTUP RESTRICT; -- OPEN but only DBAs can connect STARTUP FORCE; -- ABORT then STARTUP (equivalent to kill+restart)
SHUTDOWN MODES
ModeBehaviorSQL Server Equiv
NORMALWaits for all sessions to disconnect; no new connections allowedSHUTDOWN WITH NOWAIT (gentle)
TRANSACTIONALWaits for active transactions to finish; disconnects idle sessionsSHUTDOWN (WITH NO_WAIT for idle)
IMMEDIATERolls back active transactions, disconnects all — most commonNET STOP MSSQLSERVER (normal)
ABORTKills everything immediately — instance recovery at next startupKill sqlservr.exe (crash-like)
Use SHUTDOWN IMMEDIATE for planned maintenance. SHUTDOWN ABORT only in emergencies — requires instance recovery on next startup (like SQL Server crash recovery).
DAY 2 · M7 TABLESPACES & STORAGE HIERARCHY
STORAGE HIERARCHY
Database
One physical database = all tablespaces together
Tablespace
Logical storage unit = SQL Server Filegroup (contains one or more datafiles)
Datafile (.dbf)
OS file that belongs to a tablespace = SQL Server .mdf/.ndf file
Segment
Object stored in tablespace (table, index, undo segment…)
Extent
Group of contiguous blocks allocated together (like SQL Server extent = 8 pages)
Block
Smallest unit of I/O (DB_BLOCK_SIZE, default 8KB = same as SQL Server page)
SYSTEM TABLESPACES
SYSTEM
Data dictionary tables — never store user data here
SYSAUX
Auxiliary data: AWR, ADDM, Enterprise Manager repository
UNDOTBS1
Stores before-images for MVCC and rollback (SQL Server: tempdb version store)
TEMP
Temporary segments for sort/hash operations (SQL Server TempDB)
USERS
Default user tablespace — store application objects here
-- Create a tablespace CREATE TABLESPACE app_data DATAFILE '/u01/oradata/ORCL/app_data01.dbf' SIZE 500M AUTOEXTEND ON NEXT 100M MAXSIZE 10G; -- Check tablespace usage (equivalent to SQL Server free space check) SELECT tablespace_name, ROUND(used_space*8192/1048576,1) used_mb, ROUND(tablespace_size*8192/1048576,1) total_mb FROM dba_tablespace_usage_metrics;
DAY 2 · M8–M9 REDO LOGS, ARCHIVE LOGS & CONTROL FILES
REDO LOG MODEL
Oracle model
Circular groups (2+ members each) — LGWR writes; when full, switch to next group
SQL Server model
Single linear transaction log (.ldf) — grows, wrapped by VLFs
Log switch
When a redo group fills — ARCn archives the filled group if in ARCHIVELOG
-- Check redo log status SELECT group#, members, bytes/1024/1024 mb, status FROM v$log ORDER BY group#; -- Force a log switch ALTER SYSTEM SWITCH LOGFILE; -- Enable ARCHIVELOG mode (requires MOUNT stage) SHUTDOWN IMMEDIATE; STARTUP MOUNT; ALTER DATABASE ARCHIVELOG; ALTER DATABASE OPEN; ARCHIVE LOG LIST;
CONTROL FILES & ALERT LOG
Control file
Binary file tracking DB structure: datafile locations, log sequence#, SCN. Equivalent to SQL Server master DB metadata
Multiplexing
Always keep 2-3 copies on different disks — loss = DB down
Alert log
Chronological DBA activity log + ORA- errors. Equivalent to SQL Server ERRORLOG
-- Find control file locations SELECT name FROM v$controlfile; -- Find alert log path SELECT value FROM v$diag_info WHERE name = 'Diag Trace'; -- Add control file copy (in SPFILE) ALTER SYSTEM SET CONTROL_FILES= '/disk1/ctrl01.ctl','/disk2/ctrl02.ctl' SCOPE=SPFILE; -- Then cold copy files and restart
DAY 1 · M6 NETWORK CONFIGURATION FILES
LISTENER.ORA — SERVER-SIDE
Equivalent to SQL Server Browser + named pipe/TCP endpoint config
## $ORACLE_HOME/network/admin/listener.ora LISTENER = (DESCRIPTION_LIST = (DESCRIPTION = (ADDRESS = (PROTOCOL = TCP)(HOST = dbserver)(PORT = 1521)) ) ) ## Control listener: lsnrctl start # Start listener lsnrctl stop # Stop listener lsnrctl status # Show registered services lsnrctl reload # Reload config without restart
TNSNAMES.ORA — CLIENT-SIDE ALIASES
Equivalent to SQL Server connection strings / aliases in SQL Server Configuration Manager
## $ORACLE_HOME/network/admin/tnsnames.ora ORCL = (DESCRIPTION = (ADDRESS = (PROTOCOL=TCP)(HOST=dbserver)(PORT=1521)) (CONNECT_DATA = (SERVICE_NAME = ORCL)) ) ## Connect using TNS alias: sqlplus hr/Welcome1@ORCL ## Easy Connect (no tnsnames.ora needed): sqlplus hr/Welcome1@dbserver:1521/ORCL ## Test connectivity: tnsping ORCL
DAY 2 · M10 DATA TYPE MAPPING
SQL Server TypeOracle TypeNotes
INT / INTEGERNUMBER(10)Oracle uses NUMBER for all numeric — no native integer type
BIGINTNUMBER(19)Or use NUMBER(38) for maximum precision
DECIMAL(p,s) / NUMERIC(p,s)NUMBER(p,s)Direct equivalent
FLOAT / REALBINARY_FLOAT / BINARY_DOUBLEIEEE 754 floats — or use NUMBER for exact decimal
MONEYNUMBER(19,4)No native MONEY type in Oracle
VARCHAR(n)VARCHAR2(n)ALWAYS use VARCHAR2 — VARCHAR in Oracle may change behavior
VARCHAR(MAX)CLOBUp to 128TB; use CLOB for large text
NVARCHAR(n)NVARCHAR2(n) or VARCHAR2(n CHAR)VARCHAR2 with CHAR semantics for Unicode
CHAR(n)CHAR(n)Fixed-length — same semantics
DATEDATE⚠ CRITICAL: Oracle DATE includes TIME (to the second)!
DATETIME / DATETIME2TIMESTAMP(n)TIMESTAMP has fractional seconds (0-9 precision)
DATETIMEOFFSETTIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONEStores timezone offset
BITNUMBER(1)No BIT type; use NUMBER(1) or CHAR(1) 'Y'/'N'
VARBINARY(MAX)BLOBBinary Large Object — up to 128TB
XMLXMLTYPENative XML type with XMLQuery support
UNIQUEIDENTIFIERRAW(16)Or use VARCHAR2(36) for formatted GUID strings
ROWVERSION / TIMESTAMPORA_ROWSCN or ROWIDDifferent mechanism for row versioning
⚠ CRITICAL GOTCHA: Oracle DATE stores both date AND time. SELECT SYSDATE always returns date+time. Use TRUNC(date_col) to get date-only. SQL Server DATE is date-only — developers migrating queries must add TRUNC() to avoid time-component mismatches.
DAY 2 · M11 CORE SQL SYNTAX DIFFERENCES
OperationSQL Server (T-SQL)OracleNote
Current date/timeGETDATE()SYSDATESYSDATE = date+time; SYSTIMESTAMP = with fractional seconds+TZ
Replace NULLISNULL(col, 0)NVL(col, 0)NVL2(col, not_null_val, null_val) for 3-arg version
Coalesce first non-nullCOALESCE(a,b,c)COALESCE(a,b,c)ANSI standard — works in both
String concat'Hello' + ' World''Hello' || ' World'Use || not + in Oracle
Top N rowsSELECT TOP 10 ...... FETCH FIRST 10 ROWS ONLYPre-12c: WHERE ROWNUM <= 10
Select constantSELECT 1SELECT 1 FROM DUALDUAL is a 1-row dummy table — required pre-12c
Auto-increment IDid INT IDENTITY(1,1)id NUMBER GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITYPre-12c: use SEQUENCE + trigger
If/else expressionCASE WHEN ... ELSE ... ENDCASE WHEN ... ELSE ... ENDANSI — identical; Oracle also has DECODE()
Convert to stringCONVERT(VARCHAR,col,format)TO_CHAR(col,'YYYY-MM-DD')TO_DATE() for string→date; TO_NUMBER() for string→number
String lengthLEN(str)LENGTH(str)LENGTHB() for byte length
SubstringSUBSTRING(str,start,len)SUBSTR(str,start,len)Same semantics, different name
Modulo10 % 3MOD(10,3)% operator not supported in Oracle
Empty string'' is NOT NULL'' IS NULL (treated as NULL)CRITICAL DIFFERENCE — breaks many string checks
Print debugPRINT 'message'DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('message')Need SET SERVEROUTPUT ON in SQL*Plus
Temp tableCREATE TABLE #temp (...)CREATE GLOBAL TEMPORARY TABLE t (...)GTTs persist across sessions; data does not
RowidNo equivalentROWID pseudo-columnPhysical row address — fastest single-row access
DAY 2 · M12 DDL & DML SIDE-BY-SIDE
CREATE TABLE COMPARISON
SQL SERVER
CREATE TABLE employees ( emp_id INT IDENTITY(1,1) PRIMARY KEY, last_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, hire_date DATE, salary DECIMAL(10,2), dept_id INT FOREIGN KEY REFERENCES depts(id) );
ORACLE (12c+)
CREATE TABLE employees ( emp_id NUMBER GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY, last_name VARCHAR2(50) NOT NULL, hire_date DATE, salary NUMBER(10,2), dept_id NUMBER REFERENCES depts(id) );
SEQUENCES & MULTI-ROW INSERT
-- Create a sequence (pre-12c identity alternative) CREATE SEQUENCE emp_seq START WITH 1 INCREMENT BY 1 NOCACHE NOCYCLE; -- Use sequence in INSERT INSERT INTO employees (emp_id, last_name) VALUES (emp_seq.NEXTVAL, 'Smith'); -- Current value (after at least one NEXTVAL) SELECT emp_seq.CURRVAL FROM DUAL; -- Multi-row insert (SQL Server: VALUES (1),(2),(3)) INSERT ALL INTO emp (last_name) VALUES ('Jones') INTO emp (last_name) VALUES ('Brown') INTO emp (last_name) VALUES ('Davis') SELECT 1 FROM DUAL;
MERGE STATEMENT COMPARISON
SQL SERVER
MERGE target AS t USING source AS s ON t.id = s.id WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET t.name=s.name WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (id,name) VALUES (s.id,s.name) WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE THEN DELETE;
ORACLE
MERGE INTO target t USING source s ON (t.id = s.id) WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET t.name=s.name WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (id,name) VALUES (s.id,s.name); -- Note: no DELETE clause -- Add DELETE WHERE in MATCHED clause
DAY 3 · M13 TRANSACTION MODEL — THE MOST CRITICAL DIFFERENCE
NO AUTO-COMMIT IN ORACLE
⚠ CRITICAL: Oracle does NOT auto-commit DML (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE). SQL Server auto-commits by default. Every Oracle session must explicitly COMMIT or ROLLBACK.
SQL Server default
AUTOCOMMIT ON — each statement is its own transaction
Oracle default
AUTOCOMMIT OFF — DML is open in a transaction until COMMIT or ROLLBACK
DDL auto-commits
CREATE/DROP/ALTER causes an implicit COMMIT of pending DML — no rollback possible!
-- SQL Server style (ANSI) equivalent BEGIN TRANSACTION; -- implicit in Oracle (no explicit BEGIN needed) INSERT INTO orders VALUES (100,'Pending'); UPDATE stock SET qty = qty - 1 WHERE item_id = 5; COMMIT; -- explicit required! -- Rollback on error SAVEPOINT before_update; UPDATE accounts SET bal = bal - 1000 WHERE id = 1; -- something goes wrong... ROLLBACK TO before_update; COMMIT; -- commits work before savepoint
ISOLATION LEVELS
LevelSQL ServerOracle
Read UncommittedSupportedNOT supported — no dirty reads ever
Read CommittedDefault (locking)Default (MVCC — no blocking)
Repeatable ReadSupportedNot directly; use SERIALIZABLE
SerializableSupportedSupported
Snapshot (RCSI)RCSI / SNAPSHOTDefault behavior (always MVCC)
Oracle's default Read Committed uses MVCC — readers never block writers, writers never block readers. SQL Server needs RCSI enabled to get this behavior. In Oracle it's always on.
DAY 3 · M14 MVCC, LOCKING & CONCURRENCY
HOW ORACLE MVCC WORKS
Before image
When you UPDATE a row, Oracle stores the original value in UNDO tablespace
Consistent read
Readers see the version of data as of their query start time (using UNDO)
No read locks
SELECT never acquires locks — reads use UNDO to reconstruct old versions
ORA-01555
"Snapshot too old" — UNDO was overwritten before your query finished. Increase UNDO_RETENTION or UNDO tablespace size
Row-level locks
Oracle locks individual rows — NEVER escalates to page or table locks
No lock escalation
SQL Server escalates row→page→table at 5000 locks. Oracle never escalates
LOCK MONITORING & KILL SESSION
-- Find blocking sessions (equiv to sp_who2) SELECT s.sid, s.serial#, s.username, s.status, s.blocking_session, s.wait_class, s.event, s.seconds_in_wait FROM v$session s WHERE s.blocking_session IS NOT NULL; -- Show lock details SELECT l.sid, s.username, o.object_name, l.type, l.lmode, l.request FROM v$lock l JOIN v$session s ON l.sid = s.sid JOIN dba_objects o ON l.id1 = o.object_id WHERE l.block = 1; -- Kill a session (equiv: KILL 55 in SQL Server) ALTER SYSTEM KILL SESSION '55,1234' IMMEDIATE; -- 55 = SID, 1234 = SERIAL# (from v$session) -- SELECT FOR UPDATE (lock rows for update) SELECT * FROM accounts WHERE id = 1 FOR UPDATE; -- Release with COMMIT or ROLLBACK
DAY 3 · M15 PL/SQL FUNDAMENTALS
BLOCK STRUCTURE
T-SQL
DECLARE @emp_name VARCHAR(50); DECLARE @dept_id INT = 10; SET @emp_name = 'John'; IF @dept_id = 10 PRINT 'Sales dept'; ELSE PRINT 'Other dept'; BEGIN TRY -- code END TRY BEGIN CATCH PRINT ERROR_MESSAGE(); END CATCH;
PL/SQL
DECLARE v_emp_name VARCHAR2(50); v_dept_id NUMBER := 10; BEGIN v_emp_name := 'John'; IF v_dept_id = 10 THEN DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Sales'); ELSE DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Other'); END IF; EXCEPTION WHEN OTHERS THEN DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(SQLERRM); END; /
Always end a PL/SQL block with a forward slash (/) on its own line in SQL*Plus to execute it. DBMS_OUTPUT requires SET SERVEROUTPUT ON.
LOOPS AND CURSORS
-- FOR loop (most common) BEGIN FOR i IN 1..5 LOOP DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Count: ' || i); END LOOP; END; / -- Cursor FOR loop (auto-open, fetch, close) BEGIN FOR rec IN (SELECT emp_id, last_name FROM employees WHERE dept_id = 10) LOOP DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(rec.emp_id || ': ' || rec.last_name); END LOOP; END; / -- WHILE loop DECLARE v_cnt NUMBER := 0; BEGIN WHILE v_cnt < 5 LOOP v_cnt := v_cnt + 1; DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(v_cnt); END LOOP; END; /
DAY 3 · M16 PROCEDURES, FUNCTIONS & PACKAGES
STORED PROCEDURE WITH REFCURSOR
T-SQL
CREATE PROCEDURE get_employees @dept_id INT AS BEGIN SELECT emp_id, last_name FROM employees WHERE dept_id = @dept_id; END;
PL/SQL
CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE get_employees( p_dept_id IN NUMBER, p_cursor OUT SYS_REFCURSOR ) AS BEGIN OPEN p_cursor FOR SELECT emp_id, last_name FROM employees WHERE dept_id = p_dept_id; END; /
-- Execute procedure VARIABLE cv REFCURSOR; EXEC get_employees(10, :cv); PRINT :cv;
PACKAGES — NO SQL SERVER EQUIVALENT
-- Package SPECIFICATION (public interface) CREATE OR REPLACE PACKAGE emp_pkg AS PROCEDURE hire(p_name VARCHAR2, p_dept NUMBER); FUNCTION get_salary(p_emp_id NUMBER) RETURN NUMBER; g_min_salary NUMBER := 30000; -- package variable END emp_pkg; / -- Package BODY (implementation) CREATE OR REPLACE PACKAGE BODY emp_pkg AS PROCEDURE hire(p_name VARCHAR2, p_dept NUMBER) AS BEGIN INSERT INTO employees(last_name, dept_id) VALUES(p_name, p_dept); COMMIT; END hire; FUNCTION get_salary(p_emp_id NUMBER) RETURN NUMBER AS v_sal NUMBER; BEGIN SELECT salary INTO v_sal FROM employees WHERE emp_id = p_emp_id; RETURN v_sal; EXCEPTION WHEN NO_DATA_FOUND THEN RETURN NULL; END get_salary; END emp_pkg; / -- Call package members EXEC emp_pkg.hire('Wilson', 20); SELECT emp_pkg.get_salary(101) FROM DUAL;
DAY 3 · M17 ERROR HANDLING & TRIGGERS
EXCEPTION HANDLING
ExceptionConditionT-SQL Equivalent
NO_DATA_FOUNDSELECT INTO returns 0 rows@@ROWCOUNT = 0 after SELECT
TOO_MANY_ROWSSELECT INTO returns >1 rowUse cursor or TOP 1
DUP_VAL_ON_INDEXUnique constraint violationError 2627 (unique violation)
VALUE_ERRORType conversion or size mismatchConversion error
ZERO_DIVIDEDivision by zeroError 8134
OTHERSCatch-all (like T-SQL CATCH)CATCH block
DECLARE v_sal NUMBER; BEGIN SELECT salary INTO v_sal -- ORA error if 0 or >1 rows FROM employees WHERE dept_id = 999; EXCEPTION WHEN NO_DATA_FOUND THEN DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('No employee found'); WHEN TOO_MANY_ROWS THEN DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Multiple rows - use cursor'); WHEN OTHERS THEN DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Error: ' || SQLERRM); RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR(-20001,'Custom error msg'); END; /
TRIGGERS — :OLD AND :NEW
T-SQL (inserted/deleted)
CREATE TRIGGER trg_emp_audit ON employees AFTER UPDATE AS BEGIN INSERT INTO audit_log(emp_id, old_sal, new_sal) SELECT d.emp_id, d.salary, i.salary FROM deleted d JOIN inserted i ON d.emp_id = i.emp_id; END;
PL/SQL (:OLD/:NEW)
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg_emp_audit BEFORE UPDATE ON employees FOR EACH ROW BEGIN INSERT INTO audit_log( emp_id, old_sal, new_sal, chg_date) VALUES( :OLD.emp_id, :OLD.salary, :NEW.salary, SYSDATE); END; / -- BEFORE = fires before change (can modify :NEW) -- AFTER = fires after change -- FOR EACH ROW = row-level (T-SQL default) -- No FOR EACH ROW = statement-level trigger
DAY 3 · M18 USER = SCHEMA — THE KEY CONCEPT
SQL SERVER vs ORACLE IDENTITY MODEL
SQL ServerOracle
Login (server level)No direct equivalent — user IS the login
User (database level)User (= Schema owner)
Schema (owns objects)User = Schema (same object!)
dbo schema (default)Objects owned by the creating user
USE databaseALTER SESSION SET CONTAINER (PDB only)
In Oracle, CREATE USER hr creates both the user (who can connect) and the HR schema (where HR's objects live). They are inseparable. HR.EMPLOYEES = table EMPLOYEES owned by user HR.
CREATE USER & GRANT BASICS
-- Create a user (creates schema simultaneously) CREATE USER hr IDENTIFIED BY Welcome1 DEFAULT TABLESPACE users TEMPORARY TABLESPACE temp QUOTA 500M ON users; -- allow 500MB of storage -- Minimum to allow connection GRANT CREATE SESSION TO hr; -- Developer grants GRANT CREATE TABLE, CREATE VIEW, CREATE PROCEDURE, CREATE SEQUENCE TO hr; -- DBA role (like sysadmin in SQL Server) GRANT DBA TO hr; -- use sparingly! -- Object privilege to another user GRANT SELECT, INSERT ON hr.employees TO app_user; GRANT EXECUTE ON emp_pkg TO app_user; -- Revoke REVOKE SELECT ON hr.employees FROM app_user;
DAY 3 · M18 ROLES, PROFILES & AUDITING
BUILT-IN ROLES vs CUSTOM
Oracle RolePrivilegesSQL Server Equiv
DBAAll system privileges WITH ADMIN OPTIONsysadmin
SYSDBAFull admin including STARTUP/SHUTDOWNsysadmin + service control
RESOURCECREATE TABLE, PROCEDURE, SEQUENCE…db_ddladmin
CONNECTCREATE SESSION only (12c+)public (connect only)
SELECT_CATALOG_ROLESELECT on all V$ and DBA_ viewsVIEW SERVER STATE
-- Create custom role CREATE ROLE app_readonly; GRANT SELECT ON hr.employees TO app_readonly; GRANT SELECT ON hr.departments TO app_readonly; GRANT app_readonly TO app_user1, app_user2;
PROFILES & UNIFIED AUDITING
-- Profile (password policy + resource limits) -- Equiv: SQL Server login policy + Resource Governor CREATE PROFILE app_profile LIMIT FAILED_LOGIN_ATTEMPTS 5 PASSWORD_LOCK_TIME 1/24 -- 1 hour PASSWORD_LIFE_TIME 90 -- 90 days PASSWORD_REUSE_TIME 365 SESSIONS_PER_USER 10; ALTER USER hr PROFILE app_profile; -- Unified Auditing (12c+) -- Equiv: SQL Server Audit CREATE AUDIT POLICY dba_actions ACTIONS ALL WHEN 'SYS_CONTEXT(''USERENV'',''SESSION_USER'') != ''SYS''' EVALUATE PER SESSION; AUDIT POLICY dba_actions; -- View audit records SELECT dbusername, action_name, object_name, event_timestamp FROM unified_audit_trail WHERE event_timestamp > SYSDATE - 1 ORDER BY event_timestamp DESC;
DAY 4 · M19 RMAN — RECOVERY MANAGER
BACKUP TOOL COMPARISON
FeatureSQL ServerOracle RMAN
Full backupBACKUP DATABASEBACKUP DATABASE
DifferentialBACKUP DATABASE WITH DIFFERENTIALBACKUP INCREMENTAL LEVEL 1 DATABASE
Log backupBACKUP LOGBACKUP ARCHIVELOG ALL
CompressedWITH COMPRESSIONBACKUP ... COMPRESSED BACKUPSET
EncryptedBACKUP ... ENCRYPTIONSET ENCRYPTION IDENTIFIED BY pwd; BACKUP ...
Catalogmsdb backup historyRMAN catalog DB (optional) or control file
ValidateRESTORE VERIFYONLYVALIDATE DATABASE / BACKUP VALIDATE
RMAN ESSENTIAL COMMANDS
-- Connect to RMAN rman target / -- local sysdba rman target sys@ORCL -- remote -- Full backup BACKUP DATABASE PLUS ARCHIVELOG; -- Incremental level 0 (= full, but tracks changed blocks) BACKUP INCREMENTAL LEVEL 0 DATABASE; -- Incremental level 1 (= differential since last L0) BACKUP INCREMENTAL LEVEL 1 DATABASE; -- Compressed backup BACKUP AS COMPRESSED BACKUPSET DATABASE; -- Tablespace backup BACKUP TABLESPACE users, app_data; -- List backups LIST BACKUP SUMMARY; LIST BACKUP OF DATABASE COMPLETED AFTER 'SYSDATE-7'; -- Validate without restoring VALIDATE DATABASE; CROSSCHECK BACKUP; -- Set retention policy CONFIGURE RETENTION POLICY TO RECOVERY WINDOW OF 14 DAYS; -- or: TO REDUNDANCY 2;
DAY 4 · M20 RECOVERY SCENARIOS
COMPLETE DATABASE RECOVERY
-- SQL Server equivalent: -- RESTORE DATABASE mydb FROM DISK='backup.bak' -- WITH RECOVERY; -- Oracle RMAN complete recovery STARTUP MOUNT; RESTORE DATABASE; RECOVER DATABASE; ALTER DATABASE OPEN;
POINT-IN-TIME RECOVERY (PITR)
-- SQL Server: RESTORE DATABASE ... WITH STOPAT='datetime' -- Oracle RMAN PITR: STARTUP MOUNT; RUN { SET UNTIL TIME "TO_DATE('2026-03-10 14:30:00', 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS')"; RESTORE DATABASE; RECOVER DATABASE; } ALTER DATABASE OPEN RESETLOGS;
FLASHBACK TECHNOLOGIES
Flashback TypeWhat It DoesSQL Server Nearest
Flashback QuerySELECT data as of a past time (uses UNDO)No equivalent; use backup
Flashback TableRewind a table to a past SCN/timestampNo equivalent
Flashback DropUndrop a dropped table from Recycle BinNo equivalent
Flashback DatabaseRewind entire database (needs FRA)RESTORE DATABASE (but faster)
Flashback TxnIdentifies & reverses a specific transactionNo equivalent
-- Flashback Query (see data from 2 hours ago) SELECT * FROM hr.employees AS OF TIMESTAMP (SYSDATE - 2/24); -- Flashback Table (rewind to 1 hour ago) FLASHBACK TABLE hr.employees TO TIMESTAMP (SYSDATE - 1/24); -- Flashback Drop (undrop table) FLASHBACK TABLE emp_backup TO BEFORE DROP; -- Show recycle bin SELECT object_name, original_name FROM recyclebin;
DAY 4 · M22 DATA PUMP — LOGICAL EXPORT/IMPORT
EXPDP — EXPORT DATA PUMP
-- First: create directory object (as DBA) CREATE OR REPLACE DIRECTORY dp_dir AS '/u01/dpump'; GRANT READ, WRITE ON DIRECTORY dp_dir TO hr; -- Full database export expdp system/pwd FULL=Y DIRECTORY=dp_dir DUMPFILE=full_%U.dmp LOGFILE=full_exp.log PARALLEL=4 -- Schema export (equiv: SSMS backup then restore to new schema) expdp system/pwd SCHEMAS=hr DIRECTORY=dp_dir DUMPFILE=hr_schema.dmp LOGFILE=hr_exp.log -- Table-level export expdp hr/pwd TABLES=employees,departments DIRECTORY=dp_dir DUMPFILE=tables.dmp
IMPDP — IMPORT DATA PUMP
-- Full import impdp system/pwd FULL=Y DIRECTORY=dp_dir DUMPFILE=full_%U.dmp LOGFILE=full_imp.log -- Remap schema (import HR data into HR2 schema) impdp system/pwd SCHEMAS=hr REMAP_SCHEMA=hr:hr2 REMAP_TABLESPACE=users:app_data DIRECTORY=dp_dir DUMPFILE=hr_schema.dmp LOGFILE=hr_imp.log -- Table-level import impdp hr/pwd TABLES=employees TABLE_EXISTS_ACTION=REPLACE DIRECTORY=dp_dir DUMPFILE=tables.dmp -- Network mode (no dump file — direct DB-to-DB) impdp system/pwd NETWORK_LINK=source_db SCHEMAS=hr REMAP_SCHEMA=hr:hr_new
DAY 4 · M23 EXECUTION PLANS & OPTIMIZER HINTS
EXPLAIN PLAN & DBMS_XPLAN
SQL SERVER
-- Estimated plan (SSMS button) SET SHOWPLAN_TEXT ON; SELECT ...; -- Actual plan with stats SET STATISTICS IO ON; SET STATISTICS TIME ON; SELECT ...; -- Query Store plan SELECT * FROM sys.query_store_plan WHERE query_id = 101;
ORACLE
-- Estimated plan EXPLAIN PLAN FOR SELECT ...; SELECT * FROM TABLE( DBMS_XPLAN.DISPLAY()); -- Actual plan (after execution) SELECT * FROM TABLE( DBMS_XPLAN.DISPLAY_CURSOR( sql_id, child_number, 'ALLSTATS LAST'));
OPTIMIZER HINTS
SQL Server HintOracle HintEffect
OPTION(LOOP JOIN)/*+ USE_NL(t1 t2) */Force nested loop join
OPTION(HASH JOIN)/*+ USE_HASH(t1 t2) */Force hash join
WITH(INDEX(ix_name))/*+ INDEX(t ix_name) */Force specific index
WITH(NOLOCK)No equivalent neededOracle: MVCC means no dirty reads ever
OPTION(PARALLEL)/*+ PARALLEL(t 4) */Parallel query with 4 processes
OPTION(RECOMPILE)/*+ NO_MERGE */ or flush cursorRe-optimize
OPTION(MAXDOP 1)/*+ NO_PARALLEL */Disable parallelism
OPTION(FULL SCAN)/*+ FULL(tablename) */Force full table scan
DAY 4 · M24 AWR, ASH & ADDM
DIAGNOSTIC TOOLS COMPARISON
Oracle ToolWhat It DoesSQL Server Equiv
AWRAutomatic Workload Repository — hourly performance snapshotsQuery Store + DMV history
ASHActive Session History — 1-second session samples (in V$ASH)Extended Events + DMV live
ADDMAutomated Database Diagnostic Monitor — analyzes AWR and gives recommendationsQuery Store Regressed Queries + DTA
StatspackOld AWR equivalent (pre-10g or non-licensed)Perfmon + DMVs
-- Take AWR snapshot manually EXEC DBMS_WORKLOAD_REPOSITORY.CREATE_SNAPSHOT(); -- List available snapshots SELECT snap_id, begin_interval_time FROM dba_hist_snapshot ORDER BY snap_id DESC FETCH FIRST 10 ROWS ONLY; -- Generate AWR report (in SQL*Plus) @?/rdbms/admin/awrrpt.sql -- Run ADDM report @?/rdbms/admin/addmrpt.sql
KEY MONITORING QUERIES
-- Top SQL by CPU (equiv: Query Store top resource consumers) SELECT sql_id, executions, ROUND(cpu_time/1000000,2) cpu_sec, ROUND(elapsed_time/1000000,2) ela_sec, SUBSTR(sql_text,1,60) sql_snippet FROM v$sqlarea ORDER BY cpu_time DESC FETCH FIRST 10 ROWS ONLY; -- Active sessions with wait event SELECT sid, username, status, wait_class, event, seconds_in_wait FROM v$session WHERE status = 'ACTIVE' AND username IS NOT NULL; -- Wait event breakdown (equiv: sys.dm_os_wait_stats) SELECT wait_class, event, total_waits, time_waited_micro/1000000 secs FROM v$system_event WHERE wait_class != 'Idle' ORDER BY time_waited_micro DESC FETCH FIRST 15 ROWS ONLY;
DAY 5 · M25 INDEXES & PARTITIONING
ORACLE INDEX TYPES
Oracle IndexDescriptionSQL Server Equiv
B-tree (default)Standard balanced tree — fast on high-cardinality columnsNon-clustered index
UniqueEnforces uniquenessUnique non-clustered index
BitmapCompressed — best for low-cardinality (gender, status). DW useNo direct equivalent
Function-basedIndex on UPPER(col) or expression — enables functional searchesComputed column index
IOT (Index-Organized Table)Table physically stored by primary key — data IS the indexClustered index
InvisibleOptimizer ignores unless hinted — test index impact safelyDisabled index (but invisible is more nuanced)
CompositeMulti-column B-tree indexComposite non-clustered index
-- Create invisible index (test without affecting prod queries) CREATE INDEX ix_emp_dept ON employees(dept_id) INVISIBLE; -- Force optimizer to use invisible index: ALTER SESSION SET OPTIMIZER_USE_INVISIBLE_INDEXES=TRUE; -- Make permanent: ALTER INDEX ix_emp_dept VISIBLE;
PARTITIONING
TypeDescriptionSQL Server Equiv
RANGEPartition by value ranges (dates, numbers)Range partition function
LISTPartition by specific discrete valuesNo direct; use CASE in partition function
HASHDistribute evenly by hash of column valueNo equivalent (SQL Server uses range only)
INTERVALAuto-creates partitions for new ranges (12c+)No equivalent
COMPOSITECombine types (e.g., range-hash)No equivalent
-- Range partitioning by date (interval auto-creates monthly) CREATE TABLE sales ( sale_id NUMBER, sale_date DATE, amount NUMBER) PARTITION BY RANGE (sale_date) INTERVAL (NUMTOYMINTERVAL(1,'MONTH')) (PARTITION p_before_2026 VALUES LESS THAN (DATE '2026-01-01')); -- Query partition directly SELECT * FROM sales PARTITION (p_before_2026); -- Partition pruning (optimizer skips irrelevant partitions) SELECT * FROM sales WHERE sale_date = DATE '2026-03-01';
DAY 5 · M26–M27 HIGH AVAILABILITY COMPARISON
FeatureSQL ServerOracle
Log shippingLog Shipping (manual)Data Guard (automated)
Sync replication HAAlways On AG — SynchronousData Guard — Maximum Availability
Async replication DRAlways On AG — AsynchronousData Guard — Maximum Performance
Read replicaReadable Secondary AGActive Data Guard (licensed)
Automatic failoverWSFC + AG listenerFast-Start Failover (FSFO) via Observer
Active-active clusteringFCI (active-passive)RAC (active-active — all nodes read+write)
Shared storage clusterFCI on WSFCRAC on Clusterware + ASM
Load balancingAG read-only routingRAC SCAN listener + services
DAY 5 · M28 DAILY OPERATIONS TOOLKIT
SQL*PLUS ESSENTIALS
-- Connect sqlplus / as sysdba -- OS auth (like Windows auth) sqlplus sys/pwd@ORCL as sysdba sqlplus hr/Welcome1@ORCL -- SQL*Plus settings SET LINESIZE 200 SET PAGESIZE 50 SET SERVEROUTPUT ON -- enable DBMS_OUTPUT SET TIMING ON -- show execution time -- Describe a table (equiv: sp_help) DESC employees; DESCRIBE hr.employees; -- Show parameter SHOW PARAMETER memory SHOW SGA; -- Run a script @/path/to/script.sql @?/rdbms/admin/awrrpt.sql -- ? = $ORACLE_HOME -- Spool output to file SPOOL /tmp/output.txt SELECT * FROM v$session; SPOOL OFF;
DBMS_SCHEDULER — JOB MANAGEMENT
-- SQL Server Agent Job equivalent: -- CREATE JOB ... ADD JOBSTEP ... ADD SCHEDULE -- Create Oracle Scheduler job BEGIN DBMS_SCHEDULER.CREATE_JOB( job_name => 'NIGHTLY_STATS', job_type => 'PLSQL_BLOCK', job_action => 'BEGIN DBMS_STATS.GATHER_DATABASE_STATS( OPTIONS=>''GATHER STALE''); END;', start_date => SYSDATE, repeat_interval => 'FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=2', enabled => TRUE, comments => 'Nightly statistics gather' ); END; / -- List jobs (equiv: msdb.dbo.sysjobs) SELECT job_name, state, last_run_date FROM dba_scheduler_jobs; -- Run immediately EXEC DBMS_SCHEDULER.RUN_JOB('NIGHTLY_STATS'); -- Enable/disable EXEC DBMS_SCHEDULER.ENABLE('NIGHTLY_STATS'); EXEC DBMS_SCHEDULER.DISABLE('NIGHTLY_STATS');
DAY 5 · M28 V$ VIEWS vs DMVs & ESSENTIAL MONITORING
V$ VIEW ↔ DMV MAPPING
TaskSQL Server DMVOracle V$/DBA_ View
Active sessionssys.dm_exec_sessionsV$SESSION
Running SQLsys.dm_exec_sql_textV$SQL / V$SQLAREA
Wait statssys.dm_os_wait_statsV$SYSTEM_EVENT
Buffer cachesys.dm_os_buffer_descriptorsV$BH / V$BUFFER_POOL_STATISTICS
Execution planssys.dm_exec_cached_plansV$SQL_PLAN
Data filessys.master_filesV$DATAFILE / DBA_DATA_FILES
Lockssys.dm_tran_locksV$LOCK / DBA_BLOCKERS
Users/loginssys.server_principalsDBA_USERS
All objectssys.all_objectsDBA_OBJECTS / ALL_OBJECTS
Table columnssys.columnsDBA_TAB_COLUMNS
Indexessys.indexesDBA_INDEXES / DBA_IND_COLUMNS
ESSENTIAL DAILY MONITORING QUERIES
-- DB status check SELECT name, open_mode, log_mode FROM v$database; SELECT instance_name, status, version FROM v$instance; -- Tablespace space usage SELECT tablespace_name, ROUND((1-(free_space/total_space))*100,1) pct_used, ROUND(total_space/1048576,0) total_mb, ROUND(free_space/1048576,0) free_mb FROM ( SELECT ts.tablespace_name, SUM(df.bytes) total_space, NVL(SUM(fs.bytes),0) free_space FROM dba_tablespaces ts JOIN dba_data_files df USING (tablespace_name) LEFT JOIN dba_free_space fs USING (tablespace_name) GROUP BY ts.tablespace_name) ORDER BY pct_used DESC; -- Find blocking sessions SELECT s.sid, s.serial#, s.username, s.blocking_session, s.event, s.sql_id FROM v$session s WHERE s.blocking_session IS NOT NULL; -- Top 5 SQL by CPU today SELECT sql_id, executions, ROUND(cpu_time/1e6,2) cpu_sec, SUBSTR(sql_text,1,60) snippet FROM v$sqlarea ORDER BY cpu_time DESC FETCH FIRST 5 ROWS ONLY;
M30 · ASSESSMENT CRITICAL DIFFERENCES — THE GOTCHA LIST
12 THINGS THAT WILL BITE SQL SERVER DBAS
#GotchaOracle Behavior
1Auto-commitOFF by default — DML sits in an open transaction until COMMIT/ROLLBACK
2Empty string'' is treated as NULL — WHERE col = '' never matches in Oracle
3DDL commitsCREATE/DROP/ALTER implicitly commits all pending DML — no rollback!
4DATE has timeOracle DATE always includes HH24:MI:SS — use TRUNC() for date-only
5FROM DUALRequired for SELECT without a table in older versions: SELECT 1 FROM DUAL
6Case sensitivityTable/column names are UPPERCASE by default — 'employees' ≠ 'EMPLOYEES' in quotes
7No TOP keywordUse FETCH FIRST N ROWS ONLY (12c+) or WHERE ROWNUM <= N
8User = SchemaCREATE USER also creates the schema — no separate schema creation
9No INT typeUse NUMBER(10) for integers — Oracle's NUMBER covers all numeric types
10VARCHAR2 not VARCHARAlways use VARCHAR2 — VARCHAR reserved for future changes
11No lock escalationOracle always uses row-level locks — never escalates to page/table
12Readers don't blockMVCC means SELECT never blocks DML and DML never blocks SELECT
QUICK ASSESSMENT — 10 KEY QUESTIONS
Q1. What Oracle view is equivalent to SQL Server's sys.dm_exec_sessions?
▶ V$SESSION
Q2. What is the Oracle equivalent of SQL Server's ISNULL(col, 0)?
▶ NVL(col, 0)
Q3. How do you kill session SID=55, SERIAL#=1234?
▶ ALTER SYSTEM KILL SESSION '55,1234' IMMEDIATE;
Q4. What does Oracle's DATE data type store?
▶ Both date AND time (to the second) — unlike SQL Server DATE
Q5. What startup stage reads the control file?
▶ MOUNT stage
Q6. Why does Oracle never need WITH(NOLOCK)?
▶ MVCC — readers never block writers; reads use UNDO for consistent versions
Q7. What RMAN command = SQL Server differential backup?
▶ BACKUP INCREMENTAL LEVEL 1 DATABASE;
Q8. What Oracle feature = SQL Server Always On AG?
▶ Data Guard
Q9. How do you return a result set from an Oracle stored procedure?
▶ OUT parameter of type SYS_REFCURSOR
Q10. What enables Oracle point-in-time recovery?
▶ ARCHIVELOG mode (= SQL Server FULL recovery model)
Component
ORACLE DATABASE
Description loading...
LGWR — Log Writer
DBWn — DB Writer
CKPT — Checkpoint
ARCn — Archiver
SMON — Sys Monitor
PMON — Proc Monitor
MMON — Manageability
MMAN — Mem Manager
ORACLE 19c/21c
SCN: 9,842,118
● ARCHIVELOG MODE
CHECKPOINT: 9,842,100
LOG SEQ: 482
DIRTY BUFS: 4%
← CLICK ANY COMPONENT FOR DETAILS
PRESS ESC TO RETURN
